title: The democratic ideal under stress: Citizen commitment to democratic rights and procedures in challenging times
authors: Christian Rauh, Heiko Giebler, Jan Paul Heisig, Johannes Gerschewski, Jianghong Li
abstract: To what extent do citizens support democratic rights and procedures in the face of major societal challenges? Drawing on a Dahlian ideal of democracy, we exploit the context of the Covid-19 pandemic to address this question with two preregistered survey experiments covering 9,000 respondents in six countries (Germany, Hungary, Japan, Poland, South Korea, and Spain). A conjoint experiment on preferences for pandemic policies reveals that citizens strongly oppose restrictions that interfere with their private lives but are largely indifferent to constraints on core democratic rights (elections, rallies, freedom of the press). A framing experiment assessing citizens’ procedural preferences, elicited using best-worst scaling, shows that expert and business input are consistently ranked higher than parliamentary votes or partisan consensus – regardless of whether Covid-19, climate change, or generic ‘societal challenges’ are at stake. We discuss alternative readings of our results and conclude that commitment to democracy does not appear particularly robust when citizens confront concrete crisis-related trade-offs, with largely similar patterns across countries. While our findings diverge from those of some other experimental studies, they are broadly consistent with accounts that point to rising technocratic preferences, concerns about stealth democracy, and risks of gradual democratic erosion rather than abrupt autocratic shifts.

title: Political power and the global expansion of the value-added tax
abstract: How does political power shape tax collection? This article addresses this question by focusing on the most prominent fiscal phenomenon of the recent decades – the rise of the value-added tax (VAT). Drawing on insights from the political economy literature, I test the argument that governments with strong legislative support raise more revenues from the VAT as an effective tool to improve public finances in the short term. Results from a global sample of democracies between 1990 and 2020 provide support for this argument. The effect is particularly pronounced among developing countries, where the VAT is often the tax type that accounts for the largest share of public revenues. These findings illustrate the role of domestic political dynamics in shaping the rise of the VAT to its status as a primary revenue source for the modern tax state.

title: The online transnationalisation of populist radical right publics
abstract: International cooperation between radical right populists has escalated over the last decade as parties and leaders worldwide increasingly establish formal and informal connections. Although studies have investigated the role of populist radical right parties (PRRPs) in transnationalisation processes, we know little about the transnationalisation of their online audiences, who are key to amplifying the circulation of PRR narratives across countries. This article therefore addresses the following research question: Which factors explain transnational online engagement with PRR communications? I argue that their digital publics play a role in constructing a transnational network for the PRR, facilitated by online spaces. I hypothesise that engagement varies according to party strategies, platform architectures, and linguistic and geographical factors. To investigate my research question, I analyse YouTube data from 2016 to 2024 of nine PRRPs in eight countries across three continents – the Americas, Europe, and Oceania. Using content and social network analysis, I find transnational engagement is on the rise, that it intensifies around public joint appearances of PRR actors from various countries, and is driven by language and content popularity. These results indicate that transnationalisation is not only catalysed by party elites but is shaped from below and by the architecture of digital platforms amidst a complex interplay of grassroots activity, algorithmic visibility, and party communications.

title: Occupational labor markets, skills, and immigration concerns: Panel data evidence for Germany
abstract: Economic crises, technological change, and labor market transformations have long shaped political attitudes, particularly toward immigration. While existing research links occupational risks to anti-immigration preferences, which specific occupations drive this relationship remains underexplored. This research note addresses this gap by examining how occupation-specific skills (OSS) – defined as the amount of skills required to work in a given occupation – moderate the relationship between occupational labor market conditions and immigration attitudes. Drawing on sociological and comparative political economy perspectives, the paper argues that individuals with high levels of OSS are especially sensitive to deterioration in their occupation’s labor market conditions. The central theoretical implication is that adverse changes in occupational labor markets generate stronger political anti-immigration reactions among high-OSS workers, including Managers and (Associate) Professionals. Importantly, OSS are conceptually distinct from other occupational dimensions used in the literature, such as task specificity, and from other skill dimensions, such as general skill. The empirical analysis draws on over 20 years of longitudinal data from the German Socio-Economic Panel (GSOEP), finding evidence consistent with the argument. The findings contribute to ongoing debates about the political behavior of high-skilled workers, a group whose attitudes toward immigration remain contested.

title: Fair enough? Citizens’ preferences on refugee distribution in the EU
abstract: How should asylum seekers be distributed across EU member states? And can EU citizens agree on the principles underlying such distribution? While scholars have proposed various fairness principles – such as allocation based on population size, wealth, or past intake – we know little about which principles citizens support. Using an original population survey in six EU member states, this study examines public preferences for distributive fairness in asylum governance. We find widespread dissatisfaction with the status quo of large asymmetries in refugee numbers and broad support for European responsibility sharing based on fair distribution. However, there are significant differences between member states in the principles that citizens support, and these preferences are also affected by the principles that would keep the number of refugees in their own country at a minimum. These findings suggest that while there is a transnational consensus on the need to move to a fairer distribution of refugees in the EU, its practical operationalisation reveals the deeper political divides on the matter.

title: Emotional Third Places: Creating Community in an Era of Increasing Exclusion
abstract: Conventional third places have declined among marginalized communities, yet what takes their place in fostering solidarity has been undertheorized. This article introduces the concept of “emotional third places” to explain how marginalized groups temporarily transform ordinary environments through collective emotional practices. Unlike frameworks emphasizing formal organization or planned resistance, emotional third places emerge through spontaneous gatherings and recurring rituals in spaces never intended for community-building. Drawing on interdisciplinary research, I identify four key mechanisms: formation through collective recognition, boundary maintenance through performative solidarity, dissolution through external pressure, and spatial transformation through collective behavior. By theorizing how collective emotions can reorganize spatial power relations through affective rather than political processes, this framework illuminates community formation across varying physical spaces. As traditional communal spaces become inaccessible to marginalized populations, understanding how these groups foster temporary but significant connections through shared emotional experiences is vital for comprehending social life.

title: Breaking Out of Low‐Effort Traps: Bureaucratic Leadership by Persuasion
abstract: This paper tests if bureaucratic leadership impacts the quality of service provision of a difficult‐to‐monitor task: learning in public schools. Using the empirical case of rural India, it exploits an administrative setup with two types of bureaucrats for the same spatial unit, the district, that either have more authority or more ability to engage in time‐intensive persuasion. Utilizing blocked randomization inference and bias‐corrected variance decomposition on bureaucratic postings linked to independent learning data from household surveys across 10 years, it shows that only those bureaucrats with less authority but more ability to engage in persuasion impact learning. Drawing on novel interview data, it illustrates how bureaucratic leaders can increase effort levels of subordinates through persuasion to overcome collective action problems rather than relying on orders and monitoring as principal‐agent frameworks would suggest. The findings illustrate that for difficult‐to‐monitor tasks managerial intensity and persistence trump formal authority.

Striking multiple balances in cross-sector collaborations: How leaders navigate institutional complexity with interdependent leadership behaviors
abstract: In cross-sector collaborations (CSCs), leaders must navigate the complex and often conflicting demands of the public, business, and nonprofit sectors. This study explores how leaders manage this institutional complexity through a multi-level framework, drawing on a case study of a tri-sector partnership developing technology for disaster management. Based on evidence from 27 interviews, observations, and documentary material, we identify the leadership repertoire and, as our core contribution, uncover four distinct types of interdependencies between these behaviors: enabling, substituting, stabilizing, and inhibiting. Our findings reveal that leadership in CSCs goes beyond adopting a single style; rather, it requires a strategic orchestration of how different behaviors interact. We identify two overarching strategies (selective targeting and sequential switching) that leaders use to combine complementary behaviors while preventing them from undermining each other. By focusing on leaders’ micro-level agency in response to macro-level sector differences, this study provides a clearer understanding of how interdependent leadership behaviors converge into what we theorize as institutional leadership in CSCs.

Divergence or nationalization? Voting across constituencies in four European countries
authors: Valentin Pautonnier, Ruth Dassonneville, Richard Nadeau
abstract: While the nationalization of elections is often viewed as a key characteristic of modern European democracies, the growing body of evidence on urban-rural electoral divergence suggests a reversal of nationalization, due to regional disparities in electoral outcomes. In this paper we study this issue by analyzing constituency-level election results from Denmark, England, France, and Germany over six decades. Using a divergence index, we uncover a trend toward increasing electoral divergence in election outcomes between constituencies in three of the four countries (not in Denmark), particularly since the 2000s. While we find evidence that non-mainstream parties and party system fragmentation contribute to this increase, even the results of mainstream parties have diverged more in recent elections. We also show that rural-urban electoral polarization is an important predictor of divergence which, alongside party system fragmentation and non-mainstream party strength, accounts for much of the temporal trend—suggesting that the growing structuring of party competition along the density gradient is a key driver of de-nationalization. Our findings challenge former analyses suggesting the inevitability of nationalization and underscore the importance of geographical divides in shaping European political landscapes.

title: Market reforms or institutional restoration? Anti-populist strategies in power
On the Estimation of Climate Normals and Anomalies
abstract: The quantification of the interannual component of variability in climatological time series is essential for the assessment and prediction of the El Niño - Southern Oscillation phenomenon. This is achieved by estimating the deviation of a climate variable from normal conditions, defined by its baseline level and seasonal patterns. Climate normals are currently estimated by simple arithmetic averages calculated over the most recent 30-year period ending in a year divisible by 10. The suitability of the standard methodology has been questioned in the context of a changing climate, characterized by nonstationary conditions. The paper contributes to the literature by proposing a regularized real-time filter based on local trigonometric regression, optimizing the estimation bias-variance trade-off in the presence of climate change, and by introducing a class of seasonal kernels enhancing the localization of the estimates of climate normals. Application to sea surface temperature series in the Niño 3.4 region and zonal and trade winds strength in the equatorial and tropical Pacific region, illustrates the relevance of our proposal.

title: Aspirational technologies of the (disabled) self: Confessional, self-examinatory, and ascetic masculinities among food delivery riders on China’s digital platforms
abstract: Digital platforms are increasingly offering new employment and social participation opportunities for people with disabilities (PWDs). This study examines the intersections of digital platforms, disability, and masculinity in China. Drawing on in-depth interviews and ethnography with 12 disabled male food delivery riders, it explores how platform-based delivery work serves as a site to negotiate masculinity and social recognition. The findings indicate that platforms not only offer relatively accessible and flexible income opportunities, enabling participants to engage in socially valued work and gendered responsibility, but also mobilize aspirational discourses that promise future inclusion and moral recognition while demanding intensified temporal discipline, bodily endurance, and the willingness to “eat bitterness.” Thus, digital platforms constitute an ambivalent space where inclusion and constraint coexist, enabling gendered self-formation while introducing new forms of discipline. Ultimately, this study calls for greater attention to the challenges faced by PWDs in digital labor.

title: Learning the algorithm: Responsibilization and the limits of agency on YouTube
abstract: Content creators invest extensive effort in “learning the algorithm” to secure visibility and growth. While existing research documents the proliferation of folk theories and optimization strategies, less attention has been paid to the structural conditions under which such learning unfolds. Drawing on a computational analysis of 179 YouTube videos discussing the platform’s algorithm, this article identifies recurring interpretive patterns that frame visibility as optimizable through metrics, strategic adjustment, and disciplined self-management. This paper argues that attempts at algorithmic learning are sustained by an asymmetry between epistemic agency – the capacity to interpret metrics and hypothesize about system behavior, and effective agency – the capacity to reliably influence outcomes. Because large-scale machine-learning systems are probabilistic, multi-objective, and continuously recalibrated, effective control remains systemically indeterminate. This asymmetry produces governance through responsibilization: creators internalize responsibility for unstable outcomes, sustaining engagement because the system appears responsive while remaining structurally uncontrollable.

title: Overconfident youth, underconfident elders: Age differences in algorithmic knowledge confidence and information perceptions
abstract: Age-related differences in algorithmic knowledge are typically interpreted as competence gaps that render younger “digital natives” better equipped and older users more vulnerable in algorithmically curated environments. This study challenges these assumptions by examining (a) algorithmic knowledge confidence—whether users’ subjective algorithmic knowledge exceeds or falls short of their algorithmic awareness and knowledge—and (b) what consequences such misalignment has for information environment perceptions. Using US survey data ( N = 1205), this study found systematic misalignment between subjective and awareness-based algorithmic knowledge: younger users overestimate their understanding, whereas older users underestimate theirs. Social media use partially mediated the age–knowledge confidence relationship. Knowledge confidence, in turn, predicted perceived information reliability and diversity. These findings reveal that critical assessment of information environment is determined not by knowledge level alone but by alignment between subjective and awareness-based knowledge, highlighting the need for age-tailored interventions addressing knowledge overconfidence and underconfidence.

title: The Balkan Ratline: War Criminals Evading Justice
Deexceptionalizing not Reinventing: Genocide, Palestine, and Zionism
Museums, Geopolitics, and Relational Localisation: The Making of China (Hainan) Museum of the South China Sea in a Fishing Town
The Crisification of Europe: How Intersecting Bordering Narratives Shape the Geopolitical Imaginations of Europe in the Light of Polycrisis
authors: Dorte Jagetic Andersen, Elżbieta Opiłowska, Eeva-Kaisa Prokkola, Łukasz Moll, Sara Svensson

title: From devolution to distortion: political and fiscal constraints on locally led adaptation in Kenya
Quiet de/bordering: advocacy strategies for migrant healthcare access in Ontario
Shifting paths? The evolution of Southern European growth trajectories in the post-Eurozone crisis decade (2010–9)
authors: Fabio Bulfone, Mischa Stratenwerth, Arianna Tassinari
abstract: This article traces the growth trajectories of Southern European economies (Greece, Italy, Portugal, and Spain) over the decade 2010–9. Drawing on Comparative Political Economy (CPE) literature, we derive three propositions regarding their growth profiles, performance in high value-added exports, and employment outcomes during this period. We then empirically assess these propositions using growth decompositions of adjusted aggregate demand as well as sectoral output and employment indicators. Our results show that Southern European economies share important similarities: export-led growth has been insufficient to boost aggregate growth, stimulate high value-added exports, reverse pro-cyclical employment declines, or create high-wage employment opportunities. At the same time, these economies have diverged in their sectoral growth profiles and overall growth performance. We consider the implications of these findings for potential growth-enhancing policies in the region. In this regard, boosting domestic demand appears to be a crucial precondition for sustainable growth across Southern Europe.

title: Work in a world in transition: disruptive facts and alternative paradigms toward a new world order
abstract: In this article, Santos M. Ruesga develops a structural analysis of contemporary transformations in the global economy, focusing on their implications for labor markets and institutional configurations. The article situates current dynamics within a context of overlapping disruptions—namely accelerated technological change (digitalization and automation), reconfiguration of global value chains, demographic transitions, and ecological constraints—arguing that these processes jointly undermine the explanatory and normative capacity of standard neoclassical labor frameworks. The author identifies key stylized facts, including labor market dualization, the expansion of non-standard and platform-mediated employment, declining labor share in income, and increasing heterogeneity in productivity and wages. These trends are interpreted through a political economy lens that emphasizes power asymmetries, institutional erosion, and the decoupling between productivity growth and employment quality. Ruesga advances the need for alternative analytical paradigms integrating insights from institutional economics, post-Keynesian approaches, and socio-economic theory. In this framework, labor is conceptualized not merely as a factor of production but as a socially embedded process shaped by regulatory regimes and collective actors. The article further explores policy implications, advocating for the redesign of welfare states, labor regulation, and income distribution mechanisms to address systemic precarity and inequality under conditions of structural uncertainty. The contribution concludes by framing the current conjuncture as a transition toward a redefined global socio-economic order, requiring new governance architectures capable of reconciling efficiency, equity, and sustainability in the organization of work.

title: Jumping the Gun: The Effects of Permitless Concealed Carry Laws on Crime
abstract: In the last decade, one of the most substantial changes to firearm policy is the widespread adoption of permitless concealed carry laws. Since 2015, more than 20 states have adopted a permitless carry law. Permitless carry laws allow firearm owners to carry a concealed handgun in public without first obtaining a license and undergoing a background check. This article estimates the causal impact of permitless carry laws on crime using stacked event study estimators. I find that violent crime increases by 7% in the 12 months following the law's adoption, with increases beginning shortly after passage, or several months before the law formally takes effect. I find no evidence of offsetting deterrence in property crime. The rise in violence is driven by a 12% increase in aggravated assaults involving firearms. These effects are accompanied by a 22% decline in police enforcement of weapons law violations and modest increases in reports of stolen firearms.
journal_full: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management

title: The Fertility Effects of Reducing Implicit Childcare Subsidies: The Case of School Schedules
abstract: Despite governments’ efforts to raise fertility through family‐support policies, birth rates continue to decline in high‐ and middle‐income countries. Some policies designed for other purposes, however, may function as implicit childcare subsidies. This article examines the impact of reducing such support on fertility by exploiting a large‐scale reform in Spain that shortened the elementary school day, shifting from full‐day to morning‐only sessions. Using provincial‐level data from 1981 to 2016 and a synthetic difference‐in‐differences identification strategy, I find that the reform reduced the total fertility rate by 0.12 children per woman and delayed maternal age at first birth by approximately 4 months. The delay is significantly larger in provinces with weaker pre‐reform female labor market integration, although fertility declines are similar across labor market contexts. These findings suggest that removing an implicit childcare subsidy increased the cost of childbearing, with fertility timing particularly sensitive to local labor market conditions.
journal_full: Journal of Policy Analysis and Management

title: Review of De Bonifácio a Amorim: elementos de uma teoria social da política externa brasileira
Review of Elastic Empire: Refashioning War through Aid in Palestine
Review of The Upside of US–Chinese Strategic Competition: Institutional Balancing and Order Transition in the Asia Pacific
Measure-ability: The Paris Agreement’s Transparency Regime and the Making of the Calculating State
authors: Eva Lövbrand, Maria Jernnäs, Aarti Gupta
abstract: Transparency is at the center of the Paris climate regime and one of the key mechanisms by which it seeks to catalyze voluntary climate action. In this article, we analyze the micropolitics of transparency and the power that resides in the seemingly technical and mundane reporting provisions of the Paris Agreement. Informed by Michel Foucault’s decentered account of power and government, we trace the ways of thinking and reasoning that underpin the Paris Agreement’s push to disclose, measure, and evaluate climate performance and how its complex transparency apparatus seeks to arrange things so that states behave as they ought. We identify measure-ability as the governmentality that gives the Paris Agreement’s transparency regime a particular matrix of means and ends. It is a liberal form of climate rule, we argue, that seeks to foster measure-able states that do not need to be governed by others but will govern themselves.

title: The Show Must Go On? Revisiting the Script of International Climate Cooperation Ten Years After Paris
authors: Florian Steig, Eva Lövbrand, Angela Oels, Jonathan Barnes, Diana Eriksson Lagerqvist, Marie Fischer, Jennifer Gabrys, Aarti Gupta, Maarten Hajer, Phoebe Hamilton Jones, Maria Jernnäs, Cecilia Oliveira, Jeroen Oomen, Johannes Stripple
abstract: This special issue reflects on the state and direction of global climate governance ten years after the adoption of the Paris Agreement. Informed by Michel Foucault’s political thought and the wider field of climate governmentality studies that his work has inspired, we trace the power/knowledge regimes, political rationalities, and many practical tools and techniques that make climate change thinkable and knowable as a political problem. In this introduction, we draw upon the contributions to this special issue to examine how the Paris climate regime reinvents itself to maintain legitimacy amid geopolitical rivalry, rising anti-climate populism, and accelerating climate crises. We identify four practices that work to stabilize this governmental regime by promising future improvements, governmentalizing new frontiers, normalizing overshoot and climate disaster, and funneling climate expertise. We conclude by reflecting on the role and responsibility of global environmental politics scholarship in upholding, resisting, or rewriting this script for international climate cooperation.

title: The Promissory Governance of Climate Finance
abstract: Climate finance is central to the politics of the UNFCCC Paris Agreement. Drawing on Beckert’s conceptualization of promissory legitimacy, I argue that the reported results of adaptation finance projects facilitate claims about the future, which consolidate the political authority of the UNFCCC. Drawing on thinking from science and technology studies and the sociology of monitoring, evaluation, and learning (MEL), I explore the knowledge politics of adaptation finance. Specifically, I ask how the Green Climate Fund’s (GCF) adaptation portfolio contributes to the governance of climate change. I demonstrate how the Fund’s results processes incentivize projects to be designed for approval, rather than for implementation. The GCF Secretariat gains credibility and political authority via promissory claims about its results. Theoretically, my analysis clarifies how MEL underlies powerful claims about adaptation, despite ambiguity about the impacts of projects.

title: Remaking the Amazon? The Emergence of Transterritorial Spaces of Governmentality
abstract: Since the Paris Agreement, the Amazon has been reimagined not merely as a sovereign territory but as a critical “body” of planetary physiology. This Forum article argues that this shift represents the emergence of the Amazon as a “transterritorial space”—a zone governed by transnational flows of capital, data, and carbon metrics that override local knowledge, modes of life, and conflicts. Drawing on the work of political scientist Edson Passetti and Caribbean scholar Katherine McKittrick, I analyze how the region and its environments are being governmentalized. I propose that current climate governance functions as a rationality that reduces the Amazon to a monoculture of carbon sequestration for the benefit of global elites and capitalist mechanisms in the North and the South. As evidenced by the outcomes of the thirtieth Conference of the Parties, the danger lies in the preservation of structures crucial for the continuity of fossil fuel exploration and the proliferation of market-oriented mechanisms. These mechanisms frequently ignore urgent scientific alerts regarding deforestation, while local knowledge is legitimized only when forced to fit within the dominant rationalities underpinning global climate governance.

title: The Script of the Science–Policy Interface: Performative Routines in Climate Politics
abstract: The urgency of political action to safeguard against anthropogenic climate change is widely acknowledged. Much of this urgency stems from environmental scientists’ projections that have consistently shown dire future risks. A key part in providing such projections with political legitimacy is institutional “science–policy interface” (SPI) arrangements, which influence the ways in which environmental knowledge is produced and the ways in which political actors respond. In this contribution, building on the scholarship on the interaction between scientific knowledge and policy, we investigate the “script” of the SPI and its implicit rules. We identify seven well-established performative routines that most actors adhere to, often implicitly. Drawing on the literature on the coproduction of knowledge and governmentality, we investigate this script as a technology of governance. On the basis of secondary analysis of academic literature, prior interviews, document analysis, and autoethnographic observations, we show how these routines are upheld and challenged.

title: More Than an Empty Bucket? The Competing Rationalities in the Operationalization of the Fund for Responding to Loss and Damage
abstract: In 2023, the UN climate conference celebrated the successful operationalization of new funding arrangements (FAs), including the new Fund for responding to Loss and Damage (FRLD) from climate change. However, given how little money has been received by the fund, the FRLD has been compared to an “empty bucket.” We use a governmentality analysis based on Mitchell Dean to explore the rationalities that have competed in shaping finance for loss and damage toward specific ends, and how the fund is designed as a result. In total, four competing rationalities have been at play: optimizing, additional voluntary support, scaling finance to needs, and historical responsibility. We show that governing takes place through an empty fund aiming to empower recipients toward self-help and resilience. As questions of historical responsibility and liability have been silenced, the FRLD has been emptied of its radical potential for climate justice.

title: Do Voters Understand the Benefits of Taxes?
authors: Benjamin Carter, Alessandro Del Ponte, Peter DeScioli
abstract: We use economic experiments to study a fundamental question in political economy: Do voters understand the benefits of taxes? Participants play an economic game as members of a virtual society composed of computer players. In each round, the players earn low or high income, see the distribution of income, and then vote for low or high taxes. We test whether participants vote rationally for high taxes when poor and low taxes when rich, following a classic model in political economy, or whether they focus on the burden of taxes more than the benefits. Across three experiments, poor players voted against high taxes 30% to 50% of the time, and they were more opposed when the same policy was labeled “taxes” than when it was labeled “sharing.” In Experiment 2, Singaporean participants opposed taxes at about the same rate as those of Americans in Experiment 1. In Experiment 3, participants’ opposition to taxes persisted when they received low income every round. These results support the burden hypothesis: voters focus more on the burden of paying taxes than on the benefits they provide. We reflect on what the results imply for the politics of taxes.

title: Silencing Dissent: Academic Freedom and Transnational Suppression of Palestinian Solidarity
authors: Sevgi Doğan, Donatella Della Porta, Federico Alagna
abstract: Israel’s war on Gaza has prompted a wave of protests against human rights violations that has spread across most European countries. As universities have become arenas of contestation, there has been an increase in forms of repression of academic freedom and of political rights within academia. Our systematic, case-oriented analysis of an original database enables us to identify specific repertoires of repression employed by different institutions, as well as the characteristics of the targeted academics. By comparing Germany and the United States as case studies of high and increasing levels of repression—albeit characterized by differing systems of higher education and conceptions of academic freedom—we analyze the strategies used by these actors and how they interact. In this article, we focus on the mechanisms through which repression has been promoted, anchored in a resignification of antisemitism that treats it as distinct from racism and accords it a special status in the defense of Western civilization. We use the concept of moral panic to analyze the construction of a narrative that portrays a specific challenge to society’s fundamental values, which has resulted in the scapegoating of the Left and migrants. We also identify the main moral panic entrepreneurs responsible for the creation of this environment both within and outside universities.

title: Encompassing Authority in Election Campaign Mobilization
abstract: Where central state authority is limited, local leaders play a crucial role in political mobilization. But why are some leaders more effective than others? Dominant explanations emphasize leader characteristics, such as social centrality and shared social identity with voters, or individuals’ traits like wealth and gender. We argue that a critical factor has been overlooked: the breadth of leaders’ perceived influence across various domains of individuals’ lives. We introduce and test the concept of encompassing authority – the extent to which individuals perceive a leader as influencing multiple aspects of their lives. Drawing on original survey data from Zambia’s 2021 elections, we examine three types of local mobilizers: religious leaders, chiefs, and local councillors. We show that encompassing authority predicts mobilization in campaigns and community meetings independently of leader and individual characteristics. We identify three mechanisms that explain this effect: belief in the leader’s legitimacy, fear of sanctions for noncompliance, and confidence in the leader’s ability to coordinate collective action. By conceptualizing authority as a dyadic, relational property rather than fixed characteristics of leaders or individuals, our study offers new insights into the underpinnings of political mobilization and opens avenues for future work.

title: The Right to the Campus: Student Occupations, the University, and the Right to the City
abstract: While the 2024 Gaza solidarity encampments and campus occupations before them have been the objects of significant debate among academics and broader public audiences, the spatialized and radical democratic dimensions of these protests remain undertheorized. This article argues that turning to Henri Lefebvre provides valuable tools for better understanding campus occupations and answering the questions that universities face today. Grounded in Lefebvre’s comments about students, knowledge production, and campus life following May 1968, I argue that the university is a key site for demanding, clarifying, and expanding the “right to the city.” Extending Lefebvre’s analysis through a discussion of the spatial production activities of contemporary elite North American universities, I propose a novel “right to the campus” defined by radical democratization of university governance and use-driven spatial design. Finally, I draw lessons from the 1968 Columbia University occupations before applying the right to the campus to analyze the successes and disappointments of the more recent wave of encampments. Viewed through this framework, campus occupations posit control over university “property” as a mechanism for challenging the undemocratic management and market logic characterizing both campus spaces and university activities off campus.

title: Education and Political Attitudes: The Power and Limits of Field Theory
authors: Liesbet Hooghe, Gary Marks, Jonne Kamphorst, Julia Schulte-Cloos
abstract: A growing body of research suggests that a person’s field of education shapes their political worldview. Yet basic questions remain unanswered. How strongly is the content of education associated with political attitudes relative to income, gender, ethnicity, and, most importantly, level of education? Which political orientations are most closely linked to field, and which are not? Using original and public surveys from the United States and Europe, we show that the human-centeredness of a field is strongly related to liberal positions on a wide range of political attitudes. The association with field is comparable to, and sometimes larger than, the association with level of education. However, the theory we propose has clear scope conditions: field of education is not systematically associated with interest in politics, efficacy, participation, or affective party identification.

title: Rethinking the Deterrence-Disarmament Dichotomy: The Complex Landscape of Global Nuclear Weapons Preferences – CORRIGENDUM
authors: Lauren Sukin, J. Luis Rodriguez, Stephen Herzog

title: Rural Household Cooking Energy Transitions in the Global South: Towards Integrated User-Centered Cooking Energy Systems
authors: Omar Raúl Masera, Victor Ruiz-García, Astrid Schilmann, Montserrat Serrano-Medrano, Luz Angélica de la Sierra-de la Vega
abstract: Approximately 2.2 billion rural people in the Global South cook with solid biomass fuels. Despite decades of promoting the so-called clean fuels, the transition from biomass to these fuels in rural areas remains very limited. High fuel-price-to-income ratios, weak last-mile supply chains, single-fuel program designs, and strong culinary and cultural preferences limit exclusive use and lead to stacking with open fires. Drawing on representative rural household energy initiatives in the Global South, we conclude that systemic, integrated strategies—grounded in local cooking practices, designed to sustain long-term use, and enabling cleaner stacking across fuels and technologies, including improved biomass stoves—are more likely to succeed. Programs should prioritize participatory co-design, context-appropriate technology options, reliable supply chains and financing, and ongoing monitoring to promote sustained use. Ultimately, lasting progress in household cooking depends on combining energy interventions with broader efforts to eradicate extreme poverty and strengthen local livelihoods.
journal_full: Annual Review of Environment and Resources

title: Diasporic Futures and Transnational Hope Among Iraqis in Sweden and Denmark
abstract: Based on ethnographic fieldwork among Iraqis in Sweden and Denmark, this paper examines diasporic narratives of hope that surrounded the removal of the Saddam Hussein regime in 2003 as well as the mass protests that swept Iraq in 2019, situating this hope against an overarching narrative of despair, expressed as a simultaneous sense of stuckedness and cyclical repetition. By approaching hope as a diasporic orientation, this paper invites us to think about diasporic subjectivities in terms of affective pulls and proximities in space and time, that shift and intensify as notions of what is possible change radically in the country of origin. Moreover, I suggest, an attentiveness to diasporic narratives of hope as an affective politics broadens our understanding of the collectivities in which diasporic subjects inscribe themselves and how notions of belonging are constructed.

title: Pathways of Decline: Long‐Term Population Change in Rural Podlasie, Poland
authors: Monika Wesołowska, Adam Czarnecki, Mariusz Dacko, Agata Mróz
abstract: This study examines how combinations of local demographic, economic, infrastructural, and spatial conditions have shaped long‐term population trajectories in rural settlements in Podlasie, a peripheral region of north‐eastern Poland, between 1950 and 2021. Drawing on an adapted Chi–Ventura framework, Classification and Regression Trees (C&RT) were applied to detect patterns indicative of diverging long‐term demographic trajectories. The analysis identifies configurations of conditions associated with population decline, stability, and growth. Population decline was the overwhelmingly dominant trajectory across the study period. Agricultural dependence in 1950 emerged as the decisive determinant: villages where the majority of residents relied on farming were highly likely to depopulate, an effect reinforced by population ageing, poor housing infrastructure, and limited accessibility to large urban centres. Growth occurred only in settlements where a more diversified economic base, younger age profiles, better housing, and stronger urban connectivity were jointly present. The study concludes that long‐term population decline in peripheral rural areas is strongly path‐dependent and rooted in historically inherited economic and demographic structures. Infrastructure improvements may mitigate the decline but are often insufficient on their own to reverse it, highlighting the need for territorially sensitive policies that account for local demographic, economic, and spatial conditions.

title: Beyond Ageing in Networks: Networked Privilege and Classed Geographies of Ageing Well in Singapore
abstract: How does social class shape the capacity to age well, and what does this reveal about the structural conditions that ageing policy tends to obscure? Drawing on focus group discussions with 45 higher socio‐economic status older adults in Singapore, this paper develops the concept of networked privilege to examine how accumulated economic, social and cultural capital shapes network formation, aspiration and place‐making in later life. We engage critically with Ho et al.‘s (2024) framework of 'ageing in networks', arguing that its emphasis on older adults’ agency and unbounded geographies rests on what we term the fallacy of universal agency—the mistaken assumption that the capacity to build and sustain meaningful networks is equally available to all older adults regardless of structural position. Through three dimensions of networked privilege—aspirations, friendship and place‐making, we show how advantage is enacted and reproduced by relational configurations that appear to result from individual choice but reflect class‐based positioning shaped by Singapore's meritocratic habitus. We further demonstrate how even well‐resourced older adults face persistent vulnerabilities that networked privilege can defer but never fully resolve, pointing towards the fundamental interdependencies that characterise human ageing across class positions. The paper argues that a genuinely caring approach to ageing policy must move beyond the promotion of individual resilience towards what Tronto (2013) terms 'caring with' that attends to care as a collective, political and redistributive project rather than a matter of individual responsibility or market provisioning.

title: An Introduction to the Special Issue: Critical Camp Studies
authors: Alex Tomas Fusco, H. Nese Ozgen

title: Unpacking the Differential Impact of Development on Internal Migration Across Educational Groups of Population and the Urban Hierarchy
authors: Dorothee Beckendorff, Wenxiu Du, Mathias Lerch
abstract: This study examines how internal migration responds to development drivers across educational groups in cities of the Global South. While internal migration redistributes human capital, city‐level evidence remains limited. To better understand the urban gradients of migration by education over the human development process, we exploit census data that we aggregate to city populations, enabling a sub‐national examination of urban out‐migration across 13 countries, 313 cities and 29 census years. We apply fixed‐effects Poisson regression models to examine how out‐migration varies across education levels in response to development in the health, education and wealth domains, and how the relationship varies by city sizes. Results show that out‐migration rises with development but is strongly stratified by education. The secondary‐educated are the most responsive group, displaying the steepest increases in migration, particularly at mid‐levels of development and especially with rising wealth. The least educated group remains constrained by limited resources, while university graduates respond more slowly but in a highly selective manner, especially to local educational expansion. Developmental progress increases out‐migration in all city tiers of the urban hierarchy, with secondary cities showing consistently higher out‐migration for the highly educated. These findings nuance classic theories by demonstrating that migration transitions are stratified by education and mediated by city size.

title: Why Migrants Settle in Small Towns: Polish ‘New Immigrant Destinations’
abstract: The article contributes to literature on place attachment, migration transition and migrants' motives for settling in new immigrant destinations (NIDs). It focuses on small towns of under 20,000 population. Such towns are often claimed to be unattractive to migrants. Nonetheless, some migrants do choose smaller towns, even in Central and Eastern Europe, where many countries have only just acquired net immigration. Based on 38 interviews with migrants from different countries in two Polish towns, the article presents migrants' perceptions about the benefits of smallness, particularly the quality of social relations, and the suitability of small locations for people like them. Despite being labour migrants and refugees, many self‐identified as people who chose a small‐town lifestyle, also invoking identity factors such as age and parental status to back up their claims that the towns were just right for people like themselves. Interviewees compared their home locations in the sending and receiving countries, engaging in ‘elastic geography’ to assert and sometimes exaggerate similarities in population size. Place attachment is a process, and the study showed how some participants—particularly middle‐aged parents—increasingly identified with the small town in Poland and actively ‘made it their own’.

title: Shifting political influence? UN General Assembly behavior during debt crisis
abstract: This paper looks at the political influence that China and Western official creditors exert on how debtor countries vote at the United Nations General Assembly (UNGA). The theoretical framework emphasizes the common agency problem that both China and the West face. Starting from the premise that countries that owe more debt to China than the West are more aligned with Chinese positions at the UNGA, we argue that debt crisis changes voting behavior to be more in line with the West. Empirically, we construct a dataset of debt exposure and UNGA voting behavior covering 2000 to 2020 and perform panel analysis with two-way fixed effects. Identification is addressed by arguing that omitted variable bias resulting from political proximity to China likely has a conservative direction and a formal sensitivity analysis. We find that countries with a high debt exposure to China move towards US UNGA policy positions when they default on their sovereign debt obligations, indicating lowered political Chinese influence.

title: The politics of location and aid effectiveness: Political siting versus political implementation
abstract: This paper uses GODAD data on World Bank-funded project locations and World Bank Independent Evaluation Group project ratings to assess the impact of recipient country domestic politics on the development effectiveness of aid projects. Following recent research on the impact of spatial dimensions of recipient country domestic politics on project location selection (political siting), this paper considers how these same factors impact project implementation (political implementation). I explore how political siting and political implementation impact World Bank-funded investment project outcomes using a sample of projects approved between 1995 and 2020 and evaluated between 1998 and 2024. The analysis uncovers little evidence that political siting impacts project outcomes (likely because results are confounded by politically driven cherry picking) but does identify substantial variation in project performance linked to political implementation. This effect is only apparent when the recipient government has leverage over the World Bank because of its importance to the United States.

title: Voting the Same, Feeling Differently: Sister Circles, Black Feminist Epistemologies, and Black Women’s Political Emotions During the Harris Campaign
authors: Nadia E. Brown, Camille Burge-Hicks, Christine M. Slaughter
abstract: Kamala Harris, a first multi-racial Black woman, became the official Democratic presidential nominee on August 5, 2025. This historic achievement only amplified the intensity of discussions about Black women’s politics in the United States. As Black feminist political scientists studying Black women’s politics, we knew we needed to document the nuance, dynamism, and historical nature of Harris’s campaign. We were keenly aware that we needed to capture the narratives of Black women voters to fully make sense of this historic moment. The media narrative that Black women were excited about Harris’s nomination (Jones 2024; Walker 2024) and scholarly findings that Black women voters preferred Black women candidates (Brown et al. 2019; Philpot and Walton, Jr. 2007) seemed superficial. We believed that beneath Black women’s support for Harris lay a particularized set of emotions and wide variation in candidate evaluations, which are underexplored because Black women consistently vote Democratic and are the demographic group most likely to support Black women candidates. These varied emotive responses lead to the same political behavior: a vote for Harris. Our research examines how Black women feel about politics and how these emotions translate into political action.

title: Atmospheres and Activism: Rethinking Solidarity Politics
abstract: How and why does left solidarity fail women of color? Given the rising tide of far right extremism, this is an urgent question for social movement actors who wish to build sustainable coalitions for progressive politics. In this paper, we examine the dynamics of solidarity via a novel approach to the study of “atmospheres.” While scholars of emotions have identified the importance of atmospheres in social and political life, they are unable to answer their own important question: What do atmospheres make possible politically? We bring together the concept of racialized solidarity with the sensuous inequalities of activist spaces. We identify visual, sonic, somatic, and emotional inequalities that work together to produce what we name as the racialized atmospherics of activist spaces, in which the mood is systematically mobilized against women of color.

title: Baby Slump: The Political Consequences of Pregnancy for Candidates
abstract: Do women candidate’s parental status, whether childfree, pregnant, or a mother, influence how U.S. voters evaluate their leadership, commitment, and policy expertise at the state executive and legislative levels? Using data from a nationally representative survey experiment, I assess how voters rate these candidates’ competencies. Regardless of partisanship, I find voters reduce support for pregnant candidates, particularly at the executive level, due to concerns about time availability. I also discover a parenthood timing gap effect — while pregnancy is perceived as a limitation to do the job as an elected official, having young children as a mother enhances leadership evaluations, at least among Democrats. A partisan divide emerges, with Democrats rating maternal-cuing candidates higher on leadership competencies compared to Republicans. This research has important implications for how candidates need to strategize their presentation as we see more women and (expecting) mothers run for all types of office.

title: The Green Gender Gap in Adolescence: A Case Study of Norway
abstract: Previous research from predominantly Western contexts finds that men consistently show less concern for climate change and the environment than women, a pattern known as the Green Gender Gap. This article investigates whether political gender socialization contributes to this phenomenon. Drawing on two unique data sources from Norway, I find that among adolescents aged 15 to 18, girls are more supportive of the Green Party, consider environmental protection more important, and are more likely to prioritize the environment over oil production and industrial development. I also find that girls express greater concern about climate change than boys from as early as age 13, the earliest age at which data are available. The findings are consistent with the hypothesis that early-life political gender socialization plays a key role in shaping the Green Gender Gap. The gap emerges before structural gender sorting into different educational and occupational trajectories, and while most adolescents still live at home and share the same formative environment. These findings are particularly striking given the Norwegian context, which is characterized by minimal structural gender segregation among adolescents. The findings in this article point to gendered socialization, rather than structural differences between women and men, as one of the driving forces behind the Green Gender Gap.

title: Virtues of Regulatory Criminology
abstract: This special issue demonstrates that regulatory studies and criminology both have virtue. Regulatory criminology has neglected virtues that exceed the sum of its parts. The editors did a splendid job of pointing out in their introduction that criminology created a number of theoretical traditions that have had moments of passing value and relevance to regulatory studies. In both fields, and at their intersections, one of these traditions, an economism of rational choice, even enjoyed periods of domination. At times, this narrowed scholarly imagination has won Nobel Prizes in economics. Today economists are less present at criminology, law and society, and regulation and governance conferences. Economists must be attracted back so that intellectual renewal on offer from regulatory studies and criminology might broaden their imaginations, and they ours. Regulation and governance can benefit greatly from noting how criminologists integrate insights from micro and macro levels by fine‐grained studies of the modus operandi of lawbreakers that are connected to a historical imagination for the longue durée and a sociological imagination of macrostructures.

title: Power and Participation: Assessing Collaborative Governance of Organic Standards in Canada
authors: Amanda Wilson, Susanna Klassen, Abra Brynne
abstract: Against a backdrop of increasing political, economic, and ecological volatility, participatory governance could provide a valuable mechanism to counteract democratic backsliding caused by unchecked power and regulatory capture. Food systems in particular are a site where many interests and sectors converge—including health, agriculture, land use, labor, environment, and climate change—and where there has been increasing interest and experimentation in forms of participatory governance. This research uses the example of the organic standards governance process to understand how governance structures can enable transitions to more sustainable and just systems (including but not limited to food systems), and what we can learn from existing participatory governance approaches about how to govern complex systems in a collaborative and inclusive way. We conducted 20 semi‐structured interviews with individuals who have participated in recent organic standards review processes and analyzed key documents to characterize the sources and arenas of power at play. We found that formal authority and discursive legitimacy are enabling some participants to maintain influence at a time when increasing professionalization and resource‐based power would otherwise dominate the process. In particular, the processes in place to ensure opportunities for public comment, diverse sector representation, and limited political influence all contribute to a collaborative, participatory process. At the same time, the organic sector is facing significant challenges due to the high costs of administering such a labor‐intensive process, a lack of consistent funding, and the imminent closure of the Canadian General Standards Board as an institutional structure to support the organic standards review process. Despite these challenges, the governance of the Canadian organic standards has been a robust and unique instance of participatory governance that merits continued support.

title: “Don’t Know, Don’t Care?” How information and institutional reform affect voting in supranational elections: experimental evidence from the 2024 European Parliamentary elections
abstract: European elections consistently show lower turnout compared to national elections. Two types of explanations have been proposed. The first is the “don’t know” explanation: European citizens are not adequately informed about what the EU does, what the role of the European Parliament (EP) is, and what impact their vote can have. The second explanation is that they “don’t care”: voters do not care about European elections because they might think that EU policies do not affect them or that the European Parliament is not an important actor in EU politics. I use a between-subject survey experiment in 16 EU countries, with approximately 30,000 respondents, one month before the 2024 European elections, with a follow-up in the days after the vote, to see whether giving voters more information about what the European Parliament does and presenting respondents with hypothetical increases in the powers of the European Parliament, affects their intention to vote and eventual real-world vote. Results show that information increases likelihood to vote. Importantly, these effects last and increase actual voting propensity a month later. Surprisingly, hypothetical increases in the powers of the EP decrease the likelihood to vote among almost all groups of respondents. Among respondents that are skeptical of the EU or perceive a democratic deficit, this negative effect is more pronounced. Even among pro-EU respondents and among those that prefer to see a stronger role for the Commission, the backlash is strong. When considering increasing the powers of the EP, the European Union has little to gain and much to lose when it comes to civic participation. The EU needs to first improve its legitimacy before considering political reforms.

title: Fast Today, Fair Tomorrow: Competing Priorities Shaping EU Climate Law and Policy
authors: Alessandra Accogli, Jonas Meuleman, Aron Buzogány, Elena Fasoli, Diarmuid Torney
abstract: This review critically assesses academic literature on how EU legal frameworks and institutions have navigated the dual imperatives of urgency (“fast”) and justice (“fair”) in climate governance, since the launch of the European Green Deal (EGD) and during the first term of the von der Leyen Commission. It focuses on core legal instruments and on the roles of key institutions tasked with developing and enforcing climate obligations: the European Commission (EC), the European Parliament (EP), the Council and European Council, the Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU), and national courts. Positioned at the intersection of EU studies and sustainability transition studies, the review bridges legal and political science perspectives. Drawing on urgency and justice as analytical lenses, we identify a recurring temporal mismatch across EU climate governance, where urgency is embedded ex‐ante, whereas fairness tends to be appended ex‐post. This pattern is visible in EU climate laws—which mandate fast action through ambitious targets and accelerated transitions—and institutional dynamics. The EC and Parliament broadly align on urgency imperatives, with justice often reduced to distributive compensation. In contrast, the Council's and the European Council's intergovernmental, consensus‐driven logic emphasizes nationally framed justice, which can temper urgency. National courts strongly engage with urgency but generally defer burden‐sharing decisions to political actors. We argue that embedding justice concerns early in the policymaking process is essential for ensuring that fast and fair transitions reinforce one another. The review concludes with a research agenda to guide future interdisciplinary work on urgency‐justice dynamics amid changing political landscapes. This article is categorized under: Policy and Governance > Multilevel and Transnational Climate Change Governance

title: Temporal horizons in US climate change news
Exploring Possible Explanations for the Modest Relationship Between News Media Trust and Use
authors: Yariv Tsfati, Rens Vliegenthart, Elina Lindgren, Jesper Strömbäck
abstract: Despite the widespread assumption that trust in news media drives news use, empirical research consistently finds only a modest correlation between the two. This study investigates possible explanations for the modest relationship between news media trust and use, drawing on recent qualitative insights and testing five hypotheses using a four-wave panel survey of Swedish respondents ( N = 3,542). We examine whether outlet-specific trust better predicts news use than generalized media trust, and whether the tendency for selective exposure, news avoidance, news finds me (NFM) perceptions, and conspiracy mindset moderate the trust–exposure relationship. Contrary to expectations, outlet-specific trust did not outperform generalized trust in predicting news use. Selective exposure showed no significant moderating effects. In contrast, news avoidance significantly moderated the relationship, such that the association between news media trust and mainstream news media use was stronger for those reporting low intentions to avoid the news. Stronger associations were also observed for those ranking themselves high on the NFM and conspirancy mindset scales, though these latter interactions became insignificant when entering all interactions to the model simulteneously.

title: Economics of Tipping Points
authors: Anne-Sophie Crépin, Therese Lindahl, Aart de Zeeuw
abstract: We provide a critical appraisal of the literature related to the economics of tipping points and regime shifts. Simple modeling examples of pollution control and resource harvesting illustrate the key aspects of the economics of tipping points and regime shifts. We show how economic management should be modified to consider tipping points in deterministic and stochastic settings and how they influence common pool resources and public good provision. This review also presents relevant behavioral experimental designs for tipping point experiments and discusses further in what ways models and empirical work could better speak to each other in future research.

title: From Basic Needs to Higher Fulfillment: Resource Use and Its Impact on Sustainable Well-Being
abstract: Understanding the association between resource use and well-being is critical for addressing global sustainability challenges. Resources such as energy, water, and minerals are foundational in satisfying human needs, yet their consumption is linked to complex trade-offs across individual and societal well-being. Well-being encompasses multiple facets—hedonic (pleasure-based) and eudaimonic (meaning-based), subjective and objective, and individual and societal—all of which interact differently with resource use. This review synthesizes insights from different disciplines and identifies key patterns, discrepancies, and gaps in the literature. It recommends a holistic framework for examining how resource use can foster sustainable and equitable societal well-being within planetary limits. The review concludes by offering directions for future research and actionable policy and resource management strategies. Thresholds for over- and underconsumption can help policymakers design just policies and prioritize across well-being dimensions.

title: Structural inequality shapes the daily rhythm of stationarity and mobility

authors: Wei Zhang, Kaili Clackson, Stanimira Georgieva, Lorena Santamaria, Vanessa Reindl, Valdas Noreika, Nicholas Darby, Vaka Valsdóttir, Priyadharshini Santhanakrishnan, Victoria Leong

title: Disproportionate exposure to extreme floods in historically redlined U.S. communities
authors: Elizabeth Appel, S. Amin Enderami, Elaina J. Sutley, Admin Husic
abstract: Extreme floods and historical public policies both leave lasting impacts. We analyzed flood exposure across the neighborhoods of 202 US cities historically assessed for lending risk by the Home Owners’ Loan Corporation on a scale from ‘best (A) to ‘hazardous’ (D). Using high-resolution flood modeling and dasymetric population mapping, we found that D-graded neighborhoods bear a disproportionate flood burden in roughly three-quarters of cities. This inequality follows a systematic gradient: A- and B-graded neighborhoods are underrepresented in the flooded population, C-graded neighborhoods are proportionately represented, and D-graded neighborhoods are overrepresented. A greater fraction of D-graded residents are exposed to the 100-yr flood (6.4%) than A-graded residents to the 1000-yr flood (5.9%) with the gap widening at higher-magnitude floods. Flood disparities are most pronounced in the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic regions, aligning with the historical concentration of industrial labor and redlined neighborhoods along waterways. However, we find that proximity to waterways alone does not explain the observed inequality: flood disparities persist even among neighborhoods far from major rivers and coasts, implicating infrastructure deficits as a compounding factor. Neighborhoods marked as ‘hazardous’ nearly a century ago remain disproportionately vulnerable today, underscoring the need for equitable urban planning.

title: Population expansion of early Upper Palaeolithic hunter-gatherers triggered cultural complexity on Paleo-Honshu Island, Japan
authors: Shunsuke Totsuka, Kazuki Morisaki, Masami Izuho, Katsuhiro Sano

title: Benchmarking in public updating of economic perceptions
abstract: How do people respond to new information about social conditions? For example, does an improvement always lead people to say things are “better”? We argue that people update their views differently depending on whether preexisting perceptions are positive or negative, meaning equivalent changes in conditions can elicit different public reactions. To test this possibility, we update a classic model of aggregate public responsiveness to economic conditions by including recent public perceptions as a moderator variable. This reveals a substantial source of heterogeneity: changes in the economy matter either a great deal for public perceptions or not at all, depending on the prior levels of those perceptions. The results imply that updates of public perceptions are benchmarked to the recent past.

title: From foe to friend? Government–opposition conflict and the appointment of cabinet ministers
abstract: This paper introduces a dynamic theory of ministerial appointments in coalition governments, arguing that party leaders reward members of parliament (MPs) who fulfilled their expected roles in the prior government–opposition conflict. Using all speeches from the German Bundestag of six decades (1961–2021), we estimate MPs’ expressed positions on the government–opposition conflict dimension and assess their effect on cabinet selection. Contrary to the expectation that party leaders prefer moderate ministers to foster cooperation, we find that more loyal MPs—strong government defenders or fierce critics—are more likely to be appointed. These findings highlight how past parliamentary behavior affects future cabinet formation and suggest that ministerial appointments are shaped by partisan loyalty and role fulfillment.

title: RED: Russian Electoral Data
authors: Konstantin Bogatyrev, Georgy Tarasenko, Nikita Savin
abstract: Electoral data at fine-grained administrative levels are essential for analyzing political competition, voter behavior, and electoral institutions. Yet detailed electoral records have been notably restricted, fragmented and inconsistent in many contexts, including contemporary Russia. We introduce a comprehensive, standardized and longitudinally comparable dataset of Russian federal election results at the precinct level, spanning the period 2000–2024. Integrating digital and archival resources, the data include official records on electorate size, turnout, and voting results for all major parties and presidential candidates. The dataset is harmonized across six presidential and five parliamentary election cycles to accommodate frequent precinct renumbering, administrative boundary changes and inconsistencies in reporting. The paper describes the dataset, data sources, and harmonization process, contextualizing them within Russia’s institutional framework and research on authoritarian elections. By offering consistent and harmonized electoral returns, the dataset enables robust empirical analyses of political competition, electoral manipulation, and voter behavior in the period of Russia’s transition to a consolidated autocracy.

title: A Multimodal Dataset of Psychological, Physiological, and Behavioral Responses in Diverse Driving Scenarios
authors: Bo Chai, Mingyuan Zhang, Meichen Liu, Le Fang, Ziyi Chen, Wei Gong, Shuting Jin, Gokula Manikandan Senthil Kumar, Xingtong Chen, Stephen J. Wang

title: “You Might Like Each Other”: Mass Customization and the Structuring of Sociality Among Chinese Generation Z on the Soul App
authors: Shuyao Zhao, Linxin Wang, Yao Dai
abstract: We employed the framework of mass customization to investigate how the Soul app, a leading interest-based social platform, is reshaping sociality among Chinese Generation Z. Six months of participatory observation, from December 2024 to June 2025, in Soul’s interest-driven communities and semi-structured interviews with 11 users aged 21–29 served to explore how algorithmic mechanisms, personality tests and tag-based systems facilitate interest-driven connections. The findings reveal that Soul’s design fosters emotional support and fluid self-presentation through virtual identities yet simultaneously reinforces cultural and economic stratification by creating exclusive circles and algorithmic social cocoons. Members of Generation Z actively negotiate these constraints by deploying emotional strategies and identity adjustments to balance authenticity with platform logics. By examining algorithmic matching as an instance of mass customization in shaping sociality, this study advances the understanding of how digital natives in China navigate the tensions between personalization, scalability, and inequality in online interactions. The research contributes to platform studies by highlighting the dual role of algorithmic infrastructures as both enablers of individualized sociality and mediators of digital stratification in an individualized digital era.

title: The Political Geography of Identity: Local Determinants of Pan-Ethnicity among Asian Americans
abstract: How does one’s community shape their racialized and political identity? Research studying group-based resources and political behavior focuses primarily on the overall size of a group. This study refines our understanding of how community context shapes Asian pan-ethnic identity by examining a second dimension of population: Asian national origin diversity. Using both existing and new survey data, I find that Asians living in more heterogeneous areas view Asian identity as less important and report lower levels of linked fate. I then examine variation in the role of physical and social resources across localities that differ in group size and diversity. Respondents in localities with larger Asian populations and lower levels of diversity are more likely to report higher levels of pan-Asian salience and availability of cultural resources. My analysis highlights the role of within-group diversity and the importance of community resources and institutions in cultivating connections between identity and politics.
journal_full: Journal of Race, Ethnicity and Politics

title: Do restrictive policies suppress national identification? A comparative study of muslim and non-muslim immigrant descendants in Scandinavia
authors: Karen Nielsen Breidahl, Rasmus Sandy Harboesgaard Nielsen, Kristian Kriegbaum Jensen, Per Mouritsen
abstract: There is a widespread assumption in many Western European countries that certain immigrant groups and their descendants are reluctant to identify with the host nation. A substantial body of research has documented how factors such as religiosity, perceived discrimination and inter-ethnic contact may serve as determinants of national identification, broadly defined as the emotional and cognitive bond between self and nation. By contrast, we know less about whether restrictiveness in the immigrant policy context - e.g. the extent to which policies limit immigrants’ rights, opportunities, and access to resources - also shapes these bonds. To address this gap in existing knowledge, this study draws on unique comparative survey data collected in Denmark, Norway and Sweden in 2018 covering young adults with a minority background (both Muslims and non-Muslims) as well as a majority control group. Comparing three Scandinavian countries that differ substantially in the restrictiveness of their immigrant policy environments - yet share many structural and cultural characteristics - allows us to examine whether national policy conditions are linked to gaps in national identification between minority and majority groups. The comparative analyses reveal a striking pattern: the restrictiveness of a country’s policy environment does not appear to influence national identification gaps. Thus, Sweden - often portrayed as the least restrictive of the three countries - does not exhibit larger identification gaps than the two other countries. Moreover, the differences between Denmark and Norway are remarkably small.

title: When trust building looks like surveillance: Public polarization toward Chicago policing
abstract: Public debate about policing highlights competing views about whether community initiatives build trust or reinforce surveillance. We argue that perceptions depend on prior state orientations, which can polarize opinions across groups. Using a preregistered survey experiment in Chicago, we examined responses to the police department’s “Sentiment Dashboard,” a project designed to enhance legitimacy by collecting community feedback. Results show that the dashboard increased trust in police among non-Black respondents but reduced trust among Black respondents, especially when framed as monitoring. Among non-Black respondents, dashboard exposure also reduced support for the Black Lives Matter movement and racial equity policies unrelated to policing, such as diverse hiring and climate justice. Mechanism testing with qualitative evidence suggests that treated non-Black participants were more likely to regard police reforms as substitutes for racial equity policies, rendering further measures unnecessary. These findings reveal how state legitimacy initiatives may instead widen racial divides and weaken coalitions for structural reform.

title: Research priorities for addressing global absorbent hygiene product waste
The politics of measuring sustainability progress and citizen science
Drought amplifies the psychological burden of war
authors: Stefan Döring, Tobias Hecker, Jonathan Hall
abstract: The convergence of climate shocks and armed conflict poses a critical threat to human security and sustainable development. However, their joint impacts on human populations, particularly the erosion of psychosocial resilience, remain poorly quantified. Here, integrating georeferenced drought data from respondents’ pre-displacement regions with survey data from Syrian and Iraqi refugees ( N = 3,751), we find that the association between war exposure and post-traumatic stress is stronger among those from more drought-affected areas of origin. Under severe drought conditions, the increase in post-traumatic stress associated with war exposure was more than twice as large as under average moisture conditions. This pattern is consistent with resilience corrosion, whereby climate-induced hardship may degrade mental health and weaken the adaptive capacities communities need to navigate future shocks. These findings support policy approaches that integrate mental health into climate adaptation and humanitarian response in settings shaped by overlapping crises.
