We propose the notion of Prompt Dependency, defined as the systemic erosion of self-directed analytical capacity resulting from the habitual delegation of complex problem-solving to large language models. Users they perceive these tools as efficiency enhancers, but our analysis reveals an inversion of agency where the mastery of algorithmic interaction begins to supersede the mastery of the domain itself. Findings indicate that participants experience reduced critical synthesis and increased reliance on automated heuristics. Further observations it suggest the atrophy of foundational domain skills and a normalization of algorithmic output as a baseline for professional truth. By mapping these dimensions, we demonstrate that the efficiency gains of AI are paradoxically tethered to a long-term degradation of individual expertise. These results suggest that organizational policies must prioritize cognitive resilience or human-in-the-loop verification to mitigate the risks of structural deskilling in the age of automated cognition.

title: The Political Economy of Artificial Intelligence: Evidence from Western Europe
abstract: While advances in artificial intelligence (AI) are feared to bring about widespread job losses, workers highly exposed to the technology tend to anticipate productivity-driven improvements in their earnings and employment prospects. I argue that this paradox has important political economy implications: if labor complementarities are expected to outweigh substitution effects—raising income without commensurately intensifying employment risks—exposure to AI should weaken rather than strengthen support for redistributive policies and their political advocates. I test this argument using a combination of observational and original experimental data from Western Europe, finding that occupation-level AI exposure is negatively associated with support for redistribution, the left, and the (increasingly pro-welfare) populist right but positively associated with support for the mainstream right. The results enhance our understanding of the political economy of digitalization, suggesting a discrepancy between the perceived distributional consequences of AI and earlier automation technologies that have primarily displaced labor.

title: Stable as a rock or crumbling beneath the surface? A micro-level analysis of collective interest representation among small and medium-sized enterprises in the Netherlands
authors: Rein de Sauvage Nolting, Remko Voogd, Wike Been, Alex Lehr
abstract: As trade unions face increasing difficulties in mobilizing workers, the responsibility for maintaining socio-economic coordination has increasingly shifted to employers’ organizations (EOs) and their engagement in collective bargaining. Yet, large firms have been argued to dominate EOs, to the detriment of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). These intra-capital divisions remain poorly understood, largely due to a lack of direct, systematic evidence on SME representation. Using data on SMEs in the Netherlands (N=901) from the Sustainable SME Representation Survey (SSMERS), we provide a descriptive analysis of EO membership and perceptions of collective interest representation and subsequently address the question: To what extent can differences in EO-membership among SMEs in the Netherlands be explained by (a) the perceptions of representation, (b) organizational resources and constraints, and (c) competitive pressures? We find that the perception of EO representation by SMEs is mildly positive in absolute terms, but decidedly less positive when evaluated relative to large, multinational and export-oriented firms. The explanatory analyses suggest that among SMEs, size and resources have very limited explanatory power regarding EO-membership, nor do experienced competition, the difficulty to find and retain employees, and sensitivity to production stoppages. We do, however, find fairly clear evidence that the probability of EO-membership increases the older the company is, and that the evaluation of interest representation by EOs also matters, as EO-membership is less likely when representation is more poorly evaluated.

title: Which Poverty Are We Counting?
abstract: How much poverty the United States has, and how it compares with other wealthy countries, depends on a choice made before any counting begins: whether the poor are measured against a fixed material standard or against a moving fraction of national median income. Since the 1960s, the share of Americans in poverty fell under every fixed standard, by more than half under two of them, and rose under the relative standard. This paper reports new evidence on what each type of measure actually tracks. Across wealthy OECD countries, absolute poverty rates correlate at 0.80–0.82 with a composite index of material deprivation; relative rates correlate at 0.21–0.22, statistically indistinguishable from zero. Pointed at income inequality, the pattern reverses: relative measures correlate at 0.67–0.72 and outperform absolute lines. What a line measures is settled by whether it is fixed or moving, not by its origin or price: across fourteen fixed standards, every absolute line tracks deprivation at roughly 0.80–0.86 and no relative line reaches 0.20. The same choice moves the U.S. from 29th out of 30 countries to 12th. Relative poverty is a sound inequality statistic published under the word poverty. The paper offers three questions for telling the two apart.

title: Divergent impacts of internal migration on regional human capital in Latin America: concentration, deconcentration and limited effect
authors: Miguel González-Leonardo, Francisco Rowe, Jorge Rodríguez Vignoli
abstract: Internal migration redistributes human capital within countries. Yet, whether it amplifies or reduces regional inequalities in educational attainment remains unclear. Research shows that more educated individuals are more likely to migrate, leaving the consequences for the educational composition of origin and destination regions poorly understood. Using census data for 18 Latin American countries, we compare observed regional educational attainment with no-migration counterfactuals and combine the Duncan Dissimilarity Index, a compositional impact measure and multilevel models. Internal migration concentrates human capital in the Dominican Republic, Uruguay and El Salvador, contributes to deconcentration in Bolivia, Peru and Brazil, and has limited effects in 12 countries, including Mexico, Argentina and Colombia. Associations between migration-induced changes in human capital and regional educational attainment vary across countries, while development and income levels show no consistent relationship with these changes. Labour force participation is the only correlate, suggesting that more labour-active regions are more likely to gain human capital through migration. These findings challenge the assumption that internal migration systematically widens regional inequalities in educational attainment. Instead, its redistributive impacts depend on how national migration systems interact with regional labour-markets and contextual factors, implying that policies addressing territorial inequality must consider migration and regional development jointly

title: Ideological and Affective Polarization in Argentina
authors: Mariano Torcal Loriente, Maria Celste Ratto, Aaron Schneider
abstract: In less than a generation, Argentina has gone from being one of the least polarized democracies in the comparative CNEP dataset to one of the most—simultaneously along ideological and affective lines. For most of its democratic history, political conflict ran along a single axis: Peronism versus anti-Peronism, the grieta, largely detached from the left–right divide that organized competition across the rest of Latin America. This chapter shows how, over the past three decades, that cleavage has progressively fused with a left–right ideological axis, and how this fusion did more than sharpen disagreement—it charged it with emotion. Drawing on longitudinal survey data (Latinobarómetro 1996–2024; CNEP Argentina 2007 and 2023/24) and multilevel and OLS models, we trace this transformation to radicalization, party sorting, and rising income inequality. Our central claim is that ideological and affective polarization are not parallel trends but a single, self-reinforcing process, and that the arrival of Javier Milei is its clearest expression. The "Milei effect" pulled the entire right pole toward more extreme positions and, crucially, offered voters a crisp, unambiguous ideological signal. It is precisely this clarity that fuels emotional intensity: voters who successfully locate their party on the ideological map develop the strongest affective reactions toward their rivals. Argentine polarization is therefore not irrational tribalism but ideologically structured partisanship. In this respect, Argentina is less an exception than a vivid case of a dynamic now unsettling democracies worldwide—where the radical right supplies the ideological clarity that turns disagreement into animosity, with corrosive consequences for governance, social cohesion, and democratic stability.

title: Work and Care in Eastern and Southern Africa: Surviving Through the Fractured Family
abstract: This chapter examines the intersections of paid and unpaid work in Eastern and Southern Africa, highlighting their role in reproducing gendered inequalities. While gender disparities in labour are a global phenomenon, women in Africa face layered challenges rooted in colonial histories, fractured family structures, and limited care infrastructure. The chapter explores how women’s economic contributions straddle paid, informal, and unpaid domains, revealing how social reproduction sustains both households and broader economies. The analysis traces how colonial migrant labour systems outsourced care to rural households, disproportionately burdening women and entrenching the devaluation of reproductive labour. Contemporary evidence on labour force participation, unemployment, agricultural work, and time-use data demonstrates that African women work longer overall than men, yet much of their effort remains unpaid and undervalued. These dynamics situate women simultaneously as vital economic actors and as subjects of systemic exclusion. The chapter argues that policies focused narrowly on labour market reform or wage equality are insufficient to address persistent gender gaps, as much of women’s work lies outside the formal productive economy. Instead, more robust state intervention in social provisioning and care infrastructure is necessary to alleviate unsustainable burdens on households. By linking historical legacies with present-day realities, the chapter underscores how the unequal distribution of paid and unpaid work continues to shape livelihoods and limit gender equality in the region.

title: Crises and public support for European integration: a conjoint experiment
abstract: Crises are often treated as engines of European integration, although the public-opinion mechanism behind crisis-led integration remains underspecified. This article argues that support for EU crisis powers depends on the configuration of the crisis: its symmetry, intensity, field of impact, direct national exposure, and attribution of responsibility. The argument is tested through a crisis-profile conjoint experiment fielded in France, Germany, Italy, Poland, Spain, and the United Kingdom between 20 April and 1 May 2025. Respondents evaluated crisis profiles and indicated whether the EU should receive fewer powers, no change in powers, or more powers to address the crisis. The results indicate that military crises, high-intensity crises, symmetric crises, and crises directly affecting the respondent's country create more favourable public-opinion conditions for EU competence expansion. Fiscal and economic crises generate less support than military crises and natural-disaster crises. Attribution of responsibility mainly affects opposition to integration: when EU policies are not blamed for the crisis, respondents become less likely to prefer fewer EU powers. These findings identify demand-side conditions under which stylised crisis descriptions make European action more acceptable as protection against a common shock.

title: How Model Choice Obscures Forecast Uncertainty: The Rashomon Effect and the 2026 Senate
abstract: Election forecasts report precise probabilities. For example, as of 15 July 2026 we forecast a 41.4% chance that Democrats regain Senate control, with 49.8 expected Democratic seats (90% interval 45–55). Those predictions depend on choices we make in building our model. How much does incumbency matter? How strongly do national conditions move individual races? This article asks whether Senate electoral history (2010–2024) can tell us which choices are right. For the U.S. Senate, we show it mostly cannot. Races in the same year all move with the same national tide, so even eight cycles of polling-era elections behave more like eight observations than hundreds. We build 120 reasonable versions of a Senate forecasting model, and 117 of them predict past elections similarly well. For 2026 they put the chance of Democratic control anywhere from 33.9% to 47.1%. This is an example of the “Rashomon” effect: models starting from different assumptions arrive at similar performance but divergent substantive conclusions. We explore the consequences of this phenomena for our forecast and Senate forecasts generally. We report both our own forecast of choice alongside the “Rashomon set” of models with similar performance. In overview, the range of predicted outcomes in the Rashomon set honestly reflects forecast uncertainty in this case.

title: Ranking Data in Political Science
abstract: Across scientific disciplines, researchers use ranking data to study people's comparative judgments, preferences, and decision-making over a range of items, from medical treatments to political values. Yet existing data repositories have largely overlooked the rich and diverse ranking data collected and analyzed in social science research. In this work, we introduce a database of standardized rankings based on various studies from leading political science journals. Our first release covers 52 datasets from 27 original studies published between 2005 and 2025. Our database demonstrates notable variation in substantive topics and forms of ranking data that go well beyond canonical examples of candidate evaluations and value orientations, enabling researchers to perform comparative analyses, replication, reanalysis, methodological evaluation, and new substantive research with real-world political rankings. Along with validating our database, we illustrate it by replicating key findings in four selected studies, including those of multiple identities in American politics, the representation of Muslims in India, factional leaning in the Syrian civil war, and legislative accountability for affordable health care.

title: Applied Quantitative Analysis of R.T. Erdogan’s Multi‐Vector Foreign Policy in 2014—2022
abstract: The study is devoted to the applied analysis of the concept of multi-vector in the foreign policy of Recep Tayyip Erdogan during his presidency from 2014 to 2022. Türkiye’s foreign policy is of particular interest to researchers. Ankara is actively involved in regional and world politics. Turkish foreign policy is characterized as multi-vector, but different approaches to the definition of Turkish multi-vector are used in scientific research. The purpose of the study is to identify the main vectors of R.T. Erdogan’s personal diplomacy during his presidency. The relevance of the work lies in the application of a quantitative method that allows identifying the main vectors of R.T. Erdogan’s foreign policy on the basis of the collected database of his foreign policy contacts. The method is different in that it is free from the influence of the researcher’s personal preferences in analyzing foreign policy and the influence of individual high-profile, but isolated events. The database covers the entire period of the presidency of R.T. Erdogan from 2014 to the end of 2022 and includes all foreign policy contacts published on the website of the President of Türkiye. The developed methodology allows not only to see the main vectors of foreign policy, but also to assess their depth on the basis of mathematical calculations. As a result of the analysis, the main macro-regional vectors and countries in each of the vectors were identified, and the depth and quality of contacts with these countries were measured and presented. It was also found that the Turkish President actively interacts with the local level of government. Moreover, he implements the diplomacy of the second track. It covers the main macro-regional vectors from the side of public diplomacy. The database and the method allow us to assert that the policy of the Turkish president is indeed multi-vector, but the multi-vector nature of this policy is much deeper and goes beyond relations with countries. During the work, promising areas for future theoretical and applied research were identified.

title: More than a mirror: embedding reflexivity for transformative sustainability research
authors: Anita Lazurko, Aniek Hebinck, Romana Jungwirth Březovská, Ben Delbaere, Elizabeth Noemí Díaz General, Hans Keune, Mara de Pater, Zuzana V. Harmáčková, Paula A. Harrison, Soile Kulmala
abstract: Reflexivity is regarded as essential for enabling transformative transdisciplinary research, yet it remains a marginal practice that is weakly institutionalised. This Perspective draws on the experiences of the Reflexivity Task Force, a dedicated group of researchers established to embed reflexivity within the Horizon Europe BIONEXT project. We show that intentionally designed reflexive interventions can surface hidden biases, power dynamics and divergent perspectives influencing transdisciplinary research. However, barriers to engaging researchers with more conflictual or deeply entrenched dimensions of positionality and power can lead to ‘safe’ forms of reflection with limited impact on research outcomes. We argue that making reflexivity more than a mirror requires institutional transformation, moving reflexivity from an optional practice to a core scientific capacity embedded within research governance, funding and evaluation systems. Importantly, this institutionalisation of reflexivity must not come at the cost of maintaining its provocative spirit, which requires deliberative engagement with pluralism and contestation.

title: Promoting Sustainable Food and Clothing Consumption: A Context-Sensitive Extended Theory of Planned Behavior
authors: Shayaan Syed, Adolf Acquaye, Afef Saihi, Malik Khalfan
abstract: Sustainable consumption research often examines isolated behaviors, product categories, or consumption stages, limiting understanding of which behavioral drivers are generalizable and which are context specific. This study addresses this limitation by developing and empirically testing an extended Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) framework across six sustainable consumption scenarios in the UAE: food purchase, food utilization, food disposal, clothing purchase, clothing utilization, and clothing disposal. Conceptually, the study also argues that extensions of established behavioral theories should be framed as context-sensitive extended models rather than generic “extended” models and therefore labels the proposed framework the Context Sensitive Extended TPB (CSE-TPB) model. Data were collected from 1,263 UAE consumers spread across six context-specific surveys and analyzed using partial least squares structural equation modeling (PLS-SEM). The framework incorporated core TPB constructs in addition to personal norms, moral norms, injunctive norms, trust in businesses, awareness of government sustainability goals, satisfaction from sustainable behavior, environmental knowledge, social media influence, product availability, and perceived cost and acceptability. The findings show that the extended framework provides a meaningful explanatory basis across all six contexts, while also revealing important sector- and stage-specific differences. Overall, the findings support context-sensitive theory development and targeted sustainability policy intervention.

title: Governance Misalignment in Sustainable Consumption: Explaining the Policy-Practice Gap
authors: Shayaan Syed, Adolf Acquaye, Afef Saihi, Malik Khalfan
abstract: Global efforts to mainstream sustainable consumption and production increasingly depend on the capacity of governance systems, firms, and markets to align around systemic transformation. Yet sustainable consumption often remains difficult to operationalize, even where policy ambition and organizational engagement are strong. This study examines this policy–practice gap by conceptualizing firms not only as producers, but also as large-scale consumption actors. Drawing on institutional theory, the study introduces the concept of governance misalignment, defined as the divergence between policy expectations and the economic, institutional, infrastructural, and market conditions shaping firm behavior. Based on qualitative interviews with firms and policymakers in the United Arab Emirates food and clothing sectors, the findings show that sustainable consumption and production outcomes are co-produced through interactions among regulatory design, firm-level decision-making, supply chain capacity, infrastructure readiness, and consumer demand. Three forms of misalignment are identified: regulatory ambiguity, fragmented implementation, and competing institutional pressures. These dynamics constrain firms’ ability to move beyond symbolic compliance and limit the mainstreaming of sustainable practices, particularly where cost pressures, import dependence, weak demand signals, and uneven sectoral governance persist. The study makes three contributions. First, it advances sustainable consumption behavior research by shifting attention from individual consumer choice to systemic consumption processes embedded within firms and supply chains. Second, it develops theoretically grounded propositions explaining how governance misalignment shapes market-level sustainability outcomes. Third, it proposes a governance alignment framework comprising regulatory clarity, economic instruments, infrastructure capacity, and consumer engagement as practical pathways for more impactful and scalable sustainability transitions. By positioning firms as consumption actors, the study advances the field of research in sustainable consumption behavior (SCB) from individual behavior to systemic consumption processes, demonstrating that sustainable consumption depends on alignment across governance structures, firm-level decision-making, and consumer demand. Consequently, the study highlights how governance alignment can support the global mainstreaming of sustainable consumption and production amid accelerating climate, resource, and social challenges.

title: Barriers to Patient Agency in Health Insurer Privacy Policies: A Document Analysis of Readability, Data Sharing, and Consent Mechanics
abstract: This study examines how privacy rights are operationalized through member-facing privacy policies from five major private health insurers operating in Georgia: Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield, UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, Cigna, and Humana. Privacy policies are approached as navigational documents across three dimensions: scope (what information is collected and shared), comprehensibility (whether members can reasonably understand those practices), and actionability (whether members can realistically exercise the rights described). A systematic document analysis was conducted across ten privacy policies. Readability was evaluated using the Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level, Flesch Reading Ease, and SMOG Index. Opt-out burden was operationalized as the number of documented actions required for a member to exercise a stated privacy right, following a step-count framework adapted from Nouwens et al. (2020). Document coding was supported using a structured codebook informed by the European Data Protection Board Guidelines 03/2022 and the FTC report Bringing Dark Patterns to Light (2022). All documents exceeded the American Medical Association's recommended readability threshold for patient-facing health materials. Member-facing privacy policies described extensive collection of clinical, behavioral, commercial, and demographic information alongside broad sharing with healthcare providers, research organizations, corporate affiliates, advertising and analytics vendors, law enforcement, and in some cases correctional institutions. Among seventeen documented opt-out pathways, five described no opt-out mechanism, six demonstrated procedural asymmetry between enrollment and withdrawal, and three required written requests that insurers explicitly reserved the right to deny. These findings suggest that administrative complexity, fragmented privacy notices, and limited transparency may shape patients' ability to understand and exercise available privacy protections. Future community-partnered research is needed to examine how these documented processes align with members' lived experiences.

title: UK charities that work internationally: an open dataset with validated SDG classification and a three-way overseas engagement flag
abstract: One in ten charities registered in England and Wales says it works outside the UK. The register records where they work, but not what they work on or how: a water charity in Tanzania looks the same as a school in Nepal, and a charity running its own projects abroad looks the same as one that only raises money at home. I built an open dataset that fills both gaps for all 19,688 active internationally operating charities on the register, geocoded to their head offices. Each charity gets a primary UN Sustainable Development Goal, up to two secondary goals, and a three-way flag for how it engages overseas: operating directly abroad, funding partners abroad, or fundraising in the UK only. A large language model assigned every field from the charity's own register text. To check the tags, I hand-labelled a blind, stratified sample of 150 charities under rules fixed before labelling began. The model's primary goal matched my label 77.3% of the time (95% CI 70.0 to 83.3%), against a 56% benchmark from the nearest prior machine-learning work on the register. The engagement flag came out at 65.3%, and its errors lean one way, towards over-reporting overseas activity, so its most reliable call is "UK fundraising only". The dataset, code, validation labels, and an interactive map are public.

title: Long-term cycles in food producing archaeological regions
authors: Jacob Freeman, John M. Anderies, Jacopo Baggio
abstract: Food producing archaeological regions often display long periods of expansion in population and the productivity of technology followed by co-occurring declines--recessions. To help explain these long-term cycles, we study a general model of economic and population growth under resource constraints. The model illustrates that when pathways of innovation increase the efficiency of technology and human reproduction, a human-resource system becomes increasingly unstable. Based on an analysis of archaeological regions across scales in the American Southwest, we find that the evolution of increased technological and reproductive efficiency precede the recession of human systems. This common pathway of cultural evolution creates social instability that acts as a mechanism that favors the adoption of less capital intensive technologies and declines in population. Fundamental limits on cultural evolution create an environment that favors long-term cycles of expansion and recession.

title: Unpacking language richness as a predictor of bilingual children’s language proficiency
authors: Sharon Unsworth, Arief Gusnanto, Draško Kašćelan, Philippe Prevost, Ludovica Serratrice, Laurie Tuller, Cecile De Cat
abstract: The richness of bilingual children’s language experience is typically expressed as a composite score using parental questionnaire data. This study unpacks the concept of input richness by examining one such composite score (Q-BEx; De Cat et al., 2022b) to determine whether it reliably predicts children’s language abilities, is no more complex than required, and as user-friendly as possible. Data were collected from 173 bilingual children aged 5 to 8 across three countries (France, Netherlands, UK) with various heritage languages in each. Parents completed the Q-BEx questionnaire and children proficiency tasks in their societal language. We analysed the predictive power of the original score compared to several alternatives. Results showed (i) these alternatives were not more informative, (ii) scores including qualitative aspects of richness fared better than those with only quantitative variables, (iii) the latent variables underlying richness were comparable across languages, and (iv) whether SES was included made little difference.

title: Security Threats and Support for the Far Right: Evidence from Russian Aggression in Europe
authors: Ala Alrababah, Catherine E. De Vries, Rachel Myrick
abstract: Do external security threats reduce support for far-right parties? Existing scholarship suggests such threats should weaken extreme parties by generating rally effects, shifting attention to foreign policy, and increasing demand for experienced leadership. We develop a theoretical framework distinguishing these channels and test them in the context of Russian aggression in Europe. In Study 1, leveraging an unexpected event during survey fieldwork in the European Social Survey, we find no evidence that Russia's invasion of Ukraine reduced support for far-right parties. In Study 2, a pre-registered survey experiment in Germany, we expose respondents to a scenario of US withdrawal from NATO and a Russian invasion of an EU member state. Threats increase demand for competent leadership and boost incumbent support by about 4 points, but do not reduce far-right support, which proves remarkably sticky. Together, these findings show that security shocks can reshape mainstream competition without eroding far-right support.

title: "We Must Understand Their Needs First": Nature-Based Mental Health Programs Are Beneficial and Feasible for Older Adults
authors: Andy Hong, Talayeh M.Hosseini, Miles Elkins, Valerie Greer, Sarah Canham, Alessandro Rigolon, Michelle Sorweid
abstract: Holistic approaches to mental health that go beyond traditional medication-based therapies are often necessary for older adults (OAs). Forest bathing is a nature-based intervention that combines guided walks with mindfulness activities and has emerged as a complementary alternative to conventional therapies that promote mental well-being. However, most existing forest bathing programs are designed for the general population and may not adequately address the specific needs of OAs, particularly those experiencing mental health challenges. This feasibility study explored how forest bathing can be tailored for OAs. Using surveys, interviews, and focus groups with OAs (N = 15) and program delivery partners (N = 9), we found a synergistic effect of combining nature-based interventions with group-based program delivery. The program was seen by OAs as more than a nature experience, emphasizing improved social connection among those who experienced isolation and depression. The results highlight the importance of tailoring program content and delivery to accommodate diverse abilities, preferences, and life circumstances in later life. The study also proposes a practical implementation framework that integrates personal, social, environmental, and institutional dimensions to guide the design, adaptation, and integration of nature-based mental health programs for OAs.

title: rapidcodeR: Fast, Easy, and Affordable Data Coding with LLMs
abstract: Text-as-data methods aim to extract quantitative information from natural language. Traditionally, this was accomplished by using separate steps in each analysis, such as part-of-speech tagging, sentiment analysis, and named entity recognition. Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) hold the promise of drastically simplifying this process, as they can be flexibly instructed to extract specific information. However, transferring data to LLM providers at scale and receiving directly usable data back has previously required application-specific programming. In contrast, the rapidcodeR package offers a highly flexible approach to coding quantitative data from text at maximum speed and minimal cost. Here, we present an example of using the package to visualize international relations, directly coded from Russian UN speeches between 1946 and 2024. The results correspond well to formal alliance structures. Similar use cases can involve coding of archival information, researching social media discourse, and extracting event data from news sources.

title: Addressing Health Learning Objectives in German Adult Health Education Course Announcements: A Large-Scale Text Analysis Using Large Language Models
authors: Kerstin Hoenig, Elisabeth Reichart, Verena Ortmanns
abstract: Background Health education fosters health literacy when it addresses four complementary learning objectives derived from WHO guidance: health knowledge, beliefs and motivation, capacity and self-efficacy, and community empowerment. In Germany, Adult Education Centres (AEC) are the largest publicly funded providers of health education for adults. They aim to combine these learning objectives with practical exercise and have committed to scientific and ethical standards that exclude dubious content that is esoteric or pseudoscientific or violates the boundary between education and promises of healing. Their online course announcements reveal which learning objectives AECs communicate to prospective participants, and whether dubious content persists despite these commitments. Aims We examine how health course announcements of German AECs incorporate the four learning objectives and how prevalent dubious content is. Methods We analysed 45,788 health course announcements for courses starting between January and June 2026, retrieved from a national platform covering 386 AECs (46.6% of AECs in Germany). A locally deployed large language model (Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct) performed multi-label classification using a system prompt with code definitions and 15 few-shot examples. A random sample of 200 announcements, independently coded by three human coders, validated model output against a majority-vote gold standard. Results Practical exercise was near-universal (92%), followed by capacity and self-efficacy (47%) and health knowledge (25%); community empowerment was rare (2%). Three patterns accounted for 78% of announcements: practical exercise alone (39%), combined with capacity and self-efficacy (25%), and additionally with health knowledge (14%). Dubious content appeared in 11.5% of announcements but occurred without any other code in only 1.9%. Model performance was strongest for practical exercise (F1 = 0.96); beliefs and motivation could not be reliably coded by model or humans and was excluded from analysis. Discussion All learning objectives appear in announcements, but emphasis differs substantially; competence-oriented education beyond guided practice is less common than course volume suggests. Dubious content mostly accompanies otherwise legitimate practical offerings, yet a small cluster of purely pseudoscientific courses warrants institutional attention. The study provides an empirical baseline for health literacy promotion in adult education and a validated, scalable LLM-based approach for content analysis in health communication research.

title: Digital Transformation Capacity and Sustainable Development in South Africa’s Fintech Entrepreneurial Ecosystem: A Critical Realist Framework
authors: Veronica Motloutsi, Serestina Viriri, Alexander Samuels
abstract: Digital transformation is widely presented as a pathway to financial inclusion, entrepreneurial growth, and sustainable development, yet its developmental effects remain uneven in emerging economies. This tension is particularly evident in South Africa’s fintech entrepreneurial ecosystem, where a relatively sophisticated financial sector and expanding digital innovation coexist with persistent inequality, skills shortages, fragmented institutional support, and regulatory complexity. Existing digital transformation research has largely focused on firm-level adoption, business model innovation, and technology-enabled change, offering limited explanation of how broader ecosystem conditions shape sustainable development outcomes in contexts such as South Africa. In response, this article develops a Critical Realist Digital Transformation Capacity Framework to explain how digital transformation may contribute to sustainable development within South Africa’s fintech entrepreneurial ecosystem. Drawing on digital transformation theory, capacity development theory, entrepreneurial ecosystem scholarship, and critical realism, the article argues that digital transformation is not a self-executing technological process but a contextually mediated and capacity-dependent phenomenon. It identifies institutional capacity, human capacity, and policy capacity as the key generative mechanisms through which digital technologies may support financial inclusion, ecosystem resilience, entrepreneurial participation, and broader economic development. By integrating these literatures, the article extends global information technology scholarship beyond technology-centric and firm-level accounts and offers an African-centred, mechanism-based explanation of digitally enabled development. The framework provides a conceptual foundation for future empirical research and a diagnostic lens for policymakers, regulators, and ecosystem actors in South Africa and other emerging-market settings. The study contributes to information systems theory by introducing Digital Transformation Capacity as a higher-order theoretical construct that explains how institutional, human, and policy capacities mediate the relationship between digital transformation and sustainable development.

title: The Ancestral Origins of Public Distrust in Science
abstract: For more than 95% of human existence, humans lived in small, non-stratified hunter-gatherer bands in which survival depended on detecting deception. That vigilance, once adaptive in intimate circles, now governs how citizens evaluate vast, opaque institutions. Trust holds only while information maps visibly to reality; when that mapping fails, distrust rises. In the language of semantic information theory, trust reflects high semantic efficiency, where meaning flows predictably from signal to consequence. Distrust is not the absence of belief but the mind's error-correction response to semantic inefficiency, when words cease to align with observable outcomes. Today, digital networks spread claims faster than they can be verified, overwhelming the evolved human machinery for coherence detection. Citizens are not rejecting science; they are rejecting the impossibility of verification. The path forward is not persuasion but transparency: scientific claims must become independently verifiable by the public. A citizen signal system that is public, visible, and machine-readable would restore verification to the only layer where it has ever been cognitively stable; the layer of shared observation, where any individual can compare claims with outcomes rather than relying on institutional persuasion. Under such conditions, distrust would once again function not as rejection of science, but as rapid error correction when meaning drifts from reality.

title: Pain and Its Correlates Among Racially and Ethnically Diverse Adults with Serious Mental Illness
authors: Vedant Tapiavala, Rebecca Heller, Jonathan Werlin, Lindsey J Leininger, Molly Candon, Karen L. Fortuna
abstract: Background: Adults with serious mental illness are more likely than the general population to experience pain. However, the correlates of pain, and the role of demographic, behavioral, and psychosocial factors, are poorly understood. Methods: This one-year study included 489 respondents from several sites within a supportive housing agency spanning three New York City boroughs. Survey items included questions from several reliable and valid instruments measuring functional decline, frailty, loneliness, and other psychosocial factors. Results: Within the sample (n=489), 60.3% identified as male, 58.1% were Black, and the average age was 64.2 years old, with 60.3% diagnosed with schizophrenia and 57.1% experiencing pain. Demographic variables did not affect pain severity; however, frailty (Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient ρ = 0.49, p < 0.001), functional decline (ρ = -0.27, p < 0.001), and stress (ρ = 0.41, p < 0.001) were significantly associated with pain severity. Binary covariates within these domains, and adverse events (falls and hospitalizations), were also significantly associated with pain severity. While several psychosocial and medical factors were significant in univariate analyses, they were not significant predictors in a Tweedie regression model. Conclusions: Older adults with SMI are more likely than the general population to experience pain, which is mainly predicted by frailty, functional decline, and stress in this group. Integrated pain management models targeting this population should coordinate psychiatric care with addressing these factors.

title: Unavoidable or Unavoided? A Distinction for Loss and Damage
authors: Michael Weisberg, Koko Warner, Khadeeja Naseem
abstract: The literature on climate Loss and Damage has allowed the terms ‘unavoidable’ and ‘unavoided’ to collapse into near-synonyms, following decades of conflation in IPCC assessments and the operational literature. This paper argues that the conflation is a theoretical and policy error. Drawing on Verheyen and Roderick’s early tripartite classification and subsequent work on adaptation limits, we distinguish two structurally different kinds of residual harm. An unavoidable loss occurs when a biophysical threshold has been crossed such that no feasible effort — regardless of resources or ingenuity — could have averted it. An unavoided loss occurs when avoidance was physically possible but did not happen because the costs of avoidance were unacceptably or unjustly imposed on those least responsible. The latter concept is irreducibly normative. It requires judgement about which costs communities can reasonably be asked to bear. Treating the two as equivalent naturalizes preventable harms and obscures the corrective obligations they generate. Genuinely unavoidable losses call for retrospective support and dignitary respons. Unavoided losses ground a corrective obligation of justice. As the Santiago Network and Fund for Addressing Loss and Damage are operationalized, this distinction will determine whether institutions repair harms that could not have been prevented or remove barriers that should never have been imposed.

title: Full day schooling crime reductions concentrate on school hours: evidence from São Paulo, Brazil
authors: Augusto Alves, Bruno Pantaleão, Alei Santos
abstract: We examine the mechanisms through which full-time schooling reduces crime, exploiting the staggered rollout of São Paulo’s Integral Education Program (2012 - 2024) using a difference-in-differences estimator. Full-time school adoption reduces property crime by 18.8 incidents per hexagon-year (7%), driven primarily by robbery. Crucially, effects are temporally concentrated: robbery falls 12.1% during school hours versus 8.8% outside them, and theft falls 25.8% during school hours while increasing 4.2% outside. This pattern provides direct evidence for an incapacitation mechanism—crime reduction is largest when school supervision is most direct. Underage-perpetrator crimes are reduced disproportionately (65% for theft, 23% for robbery), indicating school-based supervision particularly deters youth. Full-time schooling operates primarily through direct supervision rather than longer-run opportunity-cost mechanisms, a finding relevant for developing country policy

title: ‘The House of Health Literacy’ - a youth-centred model of health literacy developed through co-research with adolescents
authors: Anna Wahl, Karolina Seidl, Sigrid Moick, Lisa Affengruber, Daniela Rojatz, Isolde Sommer, Lisa Stauch, Orkan Okan, Lisa Gugglberger, Ursula Griebler
abstract: Purpose: Health literacy (HL) is critical during adolescence, as this life stage is characterised by increasing autonomy in health-related decision-making alongside exposure to complex information environments. This study addresses the gap between adult-oriented conceptualisations of HL and youths’ lived experiences by co-developing a conceptual HL model grounded in young people’s perspectives. Methods: A co-research approach was applied. Youth co-researchers were recruited using purposive, convenience, gatekeeper, and snowball sampling across diverse educational and community settings. 57 youth co-researchers aged 14–22 and four adult co-researchers participated in the co-research, reaching 182 adolescents. Peer interviews, focus groups, surveys, and co-research workshops were conducted by youth co-researchers independently and together with adult co-researchers. A photovoice sub-study addressed remaining data gaps. The data were analysed using grounded theory, combining initial coding by adult co-researchers with validation meetings and participatory analysis workshops involving youth co-researchers. Results: A youth-centred conceptual model of HL—the ‘House of Health Literacy’—was developed, conceptualising HL as interconnected dimensions which, like the elements of a house, build upon and stabilise one another. Four dimensions are represented as floors of the house: (1) individual characteristics as the foundation; (2) spaces for information and communication on an online–offline continuum; (3) trust and relationships, which mediate how information is accessed and assessed; and (4) health information and health-related decision-making as the top layer, symbolising that the core HL process is built on the other dimensions. The fifth dimension, (5) society and trends, operates as an overarching influence, shaping all other dimensions. Conclusions: The Youth-centred House of Health Literacy offers a novel perspective that challenges the adult-centred conceptualisations of adolescent HL. The model aligns with dimensions of existing frameworks while adding and highlighting youths’ priorities on HL.

title: Survivors in Science: A Comprehensive Analysis of Gender Disparities
abstract: We followed “survivors in science” – scientists who started publishing in 2000 and who continued publishing until 2020-2023 (N = 41,424). They authored 2 million articles (N = 2,089,097) with more than 70 million cited references (N = 73,118,395) and worked in 38 OECD countries. Our sample is a highly selective group of successful scientists: they have succeeded in persisting in science for more than two decades. Using a raw Scopus dataset, we examined gender disparities in publishing intensity, international collaboration, journal selection, productivity, citations, team formation, and publishing breaks in 16 STEMM and social science disciplines. Several author-level metrics were computed. Our data show only a single gender gap (related to publication productivity): lifetime scholarly output gap and annual productivity gap. The data do not show a gender international collaboration gap, a gender journal selection gap, a gender citation gap and a gender team formation gap for this specific sample of highly successful individuals. Men were on average 23% more productive than women cumulatively in 2000–2023 and 19% more productive in the last 5 years studied (2019–2023). Men and women published in equally prestigious journals, received the same number of citations (field-normalized), and worked in equally sized teams. In all, 80% of scientists in STEMM disciplines and 70% in the social sciences had published every year. Our data indicate interesting disciplinary differences in gender disparities.

title: Top performers and top journals: Persistent concentration in scientific publishing
abstract: In this research, we analyze the relationship between publishing productivity and access to highly prestigious journals, treating publishing in top journals as a stratification mechanism selecting publishing elites. We study N = 144,314 Polish scientists publishing for 30 years (1992–2021) and their Nart = 433,546 unique research articles published in the period. Using bibliometric data from Scopus, we compare the scientists belonging to the top productivity decile (the upper 10%, termed top performers) and the remaining population of scientists (90%) by discipline and period (five six-year periods). We measure the share of publications in prestigious segments of journals, with particular reference to the 90th–99th percentiles, and we use nonlinear journal prestige-normalized productivity. Our results indicate that access to top journals (defined as the top 10% of journals indexed in Scopus) is powerfully and permanently concentrated in the group of top performers in all disciplines and periods studied. The differences between top performers and the other scientists are primarily of a qualitative nature: they are seen almost exclusively at the top of the journal hierarchy rather than in its bottom or middle segments. Our logistic regression models indicate the complementarity of quantity and quality: publishing intensity increases the probability of membership in the elite segment of top performers, especially when it is coupled with publishing in prestigious journals. Our results suggest that top journals function as selection gates to academic careers and that they function as durable mechanisms of elite reproduction in science.

title: Women in Science: Measuring Participation in Europe Across Disciplines, Generations and Over Time
abstract: In this research, we quantify an inflow of women into science in the past three decades. Structured Big Data allow us to estimate the contribution of women scientists to the growth of science by disciplines (N = STEMM 14 disciplines) and over time (1990-2023). A monolithic segment of STEMM science emerges from this research as divided between the disciplines in which women’s share rose most rapidly – and the disciplines in which the role of women was marginal. There are four disciplines in which 50% of currently publishing scientists are women; and five disciplines in which more than 50% of currently young scientists are women. But there is also a cluster of four highly mathematized disciplines (MATH, COMP, PHYS, and ENG) in which the growth of science is only marginally driven by women. Digital traces left by scientists in their publications indexed in global datasets open two new dimensions in large-scale academic profession studies: time and gender. The growth of science in Europe was accompanied by growth in the number of women scientists, but with powerful cross-disciplinary and cross-generational differentiations. We examined the share of women scientists coming from ten different age cohorts for 32 European and four comparator countries (the USA, Canada, Australia, and Japan). Our study sample was N = 1,740,985 scientists (including 39.40% women scientists). Three critical methodological challenges of using structured Big Data of the bibliometric type were discussed: gender determination, academic age determination, and discipline determination.

title: Towards a Theory of Performative Youth Representation: A Diagnostic Framework for Institutionalized Youth Governance
abstract: Scholarship on democratic erosion documents institutional hollowing at the national level, but its operation in the sub-national micro-sites where citizens first meet the state remains comparatively undertheorized. Formally constituted youth governance bodies routinely produce performative rather than substantive outcomes, and existing frameworks built for presidents and legislatures were not designed to diagnose why. This paper asks why youth bodies holding real fiscal and legislative power still default to performative compliance, and how the same architecture generates the tactical resistance that contests it. It introduces Performative Youth Representation (PYR), a Five-Variable Dialectical Matrix in which legal, spatial, organizational, communicative, and evaluative constraints co-produce containment and subversion within the same institutional moment. Developed through the Philippine Sangguniang Kabataan — the most fiscally and legislatively empowered youth governance body in the comparative record, hence a hardest case under the Sinatra Inference — and engaging representation theory, participatory governance, and discursive and commons institutionalism, the framework’s signature persists across three statutory regimes despite rising empowerment, indicating a structural rather than resource-based mechanism. PYR states five falsifiable propositions and three scope conditions bounding its application, offering a transportable diagnostic tool for youth governance and advancing concept formation in governance research beyond the West.

title: Identifying Symbolic Modeling of Grooming Precursors in Social Media
abstract: Short-form video platforms such as TikTok and Instagram Reels increasingly circulate dating and lifestyle advice that may normalize coercive relational scripts, framing jealousy as devotion or secrecy as intimacy. A key challenge for preventing technology-facilitated child sexual exploitation is that early grooming precursors can be communicated through audiovisual choices, such as tone, pacing, setting, and editing, that make manipulative narratives appear benign. This qualitative multimedia content analysis identified recurring observable patterns of symbolic modeling through which relational and boundary-shaping cues associated with early-stage sexualized grooming are framed as ordinary dating or lifestyle advice in public TikTok and Instagram posts. Guided by Bandura’s social cognitive theory of mass communication, a purposive sample of 100 archival posts (50 per platform) was coded in MAXQDA using a structured multimodal observation protocol. Beyond its findings, the study contributes a transferable protocol for coding relational risk across visual, audio, and on-screen text channels simultaneously, extending grooming-detection methods beyond their text-only focus. Analysis produced a taxonomy of 10 recurring masking categories, derived from 289 operationally defined codes and interpreted through four themes. Masking operated primarily through presentation-layer conventions: simulated closeness, ordinary platform packaging, staged intimacy, and instructional framing dominated the corpus. Explicit grooming-literature cues were rare, appearing in 19 of 100 posts, and two cues, secrecy normalization and isolation, never appeared. Findings may inform prevention education and support future multimodal detection research.

title: Beyond Compliance: Toward a Social Literacy Framework for Genuine Disability Inclusion in Japan and the United States
abstract: Current disability inclusion frameworks in Japan and the United States share a structural absence: the systematic education of non-disabled employees on how to interact with disabled colleagues with dignity and informed understanding. Both countries have developed legal compliance mechanisms (Layer 1) and organizational accommodation systems (Layer 2), while leaving unaddressed the social environment disabled employees enter after hiring. Drawing on 21 peer-reviewed studies and comparative legal analysis, this paper introduces the 3-Layer Inclusion Model as a novel theoretical framework for genuine workplace disability inclusion. The model proposes that inclusion requires a third, currently absent layer: the Social Literacy Layer — systematic, evidence-based education of non-disabled colleagues. Without it, legal employment produces the Dignity Gap: the distance between formal inclusion and lived inclusion — between employment and meaningful belonging. Empirical evidence from Japan confirms that stigma training significantly reduces colleague social distance (p < .05; Lem & Oyama, 2023), and that reasonable accommodation backfires when colleagues are not educated about its purpose (Handley et al., 2022). Cross-national comparison reveals that two countries with different legal architectures converge on the same Dignity Gap — indicating that legal framework design is less determinative of inclusion quality than the existence of Layer 3.

title: Intergenerational Path Dependence in Media Literacy: A Structure-Practice-Cognition Multi-Mechanistic Framework
abstract: In highly platformized digital media environments, media literacy is widely regarded as a core competence for civic participation. However, substantial and persistent intergenerational differences can be observed in media interpretation, evaluative logic, and risk perception, which cannot be readily reduced through education or technological diffusion. Existing scholarship has largely framed these disparities as remediable “skill gaps,” yet such an approach fails to account for their long-term stability. Drawing on a path dependence perspective, this study develops a multi-mechanism analytical framework integrating structural conditions, social practices, and cognitive processes to reconceptualize the generational formation of media literacy. It argues that media literacy should not be understood as a neutral and cumulatively acquired individual competence, but rather as a socially embedded outcome shaped through the interaction of technological structures, everyday media practices, and cognitive lock-in mechanisms. Specifically, technological and institutional structures configure differentiated entry points into media environments; routine practices sediment these conditions into durable digital habitus; and cognitive mechanisms—such as preference stabilization and default effects—further reinforce existing pathways. The coupling of these mechanisms produces a self-reinforcing process of generational path dependence. By foregrounding this perspective, the study offers a theoretically integrated account of the persistence of media literacy disparities and provides new insights into digital inequality and generational fragmentation in the public sphere. It also carries implications for platform governance and media education policies aimed at enhancing intergenerational equity.

title: Improving housing outcomes for people with disability: Policy and practice initiatives
authors: Monica Cuskelly, Ceridwen Owen, Damhnat McCann, Debbie Faulkner, Maryam Gusheh, Maria L Hobbs, Libby Callaway, Megan Robinson
abstract: This project sought to establish what constitutes ‘good’ housing outcomes for people with disability, the barriers and facilitators for achieving them, and policy and practice changes that could improve them. It also aimed to understand the diverse circumstances of people with disability, including populations at greatest risk of poor housing outcomes. Australians with disability remain disadvantaged in housing across several indicators, including availability of appropriate accommodation, affordability, and connection to community. The policy context is complex and people with disability must navigate multiple systems to try to get their housing and support needs met. To achieve Australia’s Disability Strategy ambition for a more inclusive nation, there is a need to focus on housing provision for people with disability and how to develop a housing system that meets their needs.

title: Family Income Volatility and Children's Education by Income Level and Genotype
abstract: Family income volatility harms children's education, and prior research finds the penalty is concentrated not among poor households but among middle- and high-income ones. Income instability disproportionately hurts children whose parents are, in stable times, best positioned to invest in their education, a pattern that remains poorly understood. We argue that income volatility disrupts parental strategies: compensation and boosting, through which parents with more resources shape their children's educational trajectories according to their genetic propensity for education. Using genotyped parent-offspring trios from the UK Millennium Cohort Study, we combine repeated measures of household income and income dynamics with children's polygenic index for educational attainment to predict adolescents’ secondary school test scores, controlling for parents' genotype to partial out genetic nurture. We find that, among middle-income families, the volatility penalty concentrates among children with low genetic propensity, consistent with a missing compensation mechanism. Among high-income families, the penalty falls instead on children with average-to-high propensities, consistent with a missing boosting mechanism. These findings suggest that income volatility does not simply destabilize resources, but it disables the specific strategies that advantaged parents use to reproduce their advantages across generations.

title: After the Stall: Botswana’s Resumed Fertility Decline
abstract: After almost a decade of stagnation, fertility in Botswana resumed declining and is approaching replacement level. Though fertility stalls are assumed to be temporary, most of the literature has focused on explaining their causes. Less is known about how fertility changes after a stall, and register data in Botswana presents an unprecedented opportunity to study post-stall fertility with universal coverage. Using descriptive analyses and a finite mixture modelling approach, I examine the trends in childbirth between 2011 and 2023. Results show that the timing of childbirth is becoming more heterogeneous and stratified. Highly educated women are postponing first births while less educated women are accelerating them. Most children are born to unmarried mothers, and this share has increased. Fertility trends in Botswana are largely comparable to other late-transition contexts, and it may serve as an illustrative case for how other stalled countries will continue their transitions.

title: The Geographic Roots of Black Partisanship
authors: Marcel Roman, Stephanie Ternullo, Michael Strawbridge
abstract: We further explain the puzzle of steadfast Black Democratic partisanship by providing new theory and evidence on the role of racial segregation. Using large surveys and an instrumental variables approach, we demonstrate racial segregation reduces Black Republican partisanship. These effects are driven by racial segregation preventing Black ideological conservatives from identifying with the Republican party instead of the Democratic party. We push the literature forward by showing this relationship is net of partisan segregation and alternative mechanisms that could strengthen Black Democratic partisanship such as limited political knowledge and group consciousness, underscoring the role of social constraint. Our theory and evidence further establishes that the roots of Black Democratic partisanship are geographic in origin.

title: Who Reaches the NBA Draft? Dual Concentration in College and High-School Pathways (2016–2026)
abstract: Public debate about who reaches the NBA draft still mixes anecdote about prep factories, suburban publics, and “kids from nowhere.” We assemble a complete finishing-school panel of 656 NBA draft picks (2016–2026)—typed into US public, local private, elite prep, and international pathways—and document dual concentration at both ends of the same file: Duke and Kentucky alone account for 9.5% of picks, the top ten college priors for 25.0%, and four elite prep feeders (Montverde, IMG, Oak Hill, Link) for 8.5%. Only 34.0% of picks finish at a US public high school; elite prep (24.8%) and local private (20.3%) together account for 45.1%, and with international pathways (20.9%) nearly two-thirds of draftees do not finish at a US public school. As a supporting ecological finding—not a claim about family poverty—under a transparent rule (public finishing school in a Census place below 0.85× state median household income), 60 / 656 = 9.1% of picks follow a clearly poor-town public path (Layer A). Broader below-state public towns raise the share to 17.8% (Layer B); private/prep campuses in poor Census places (13.6%, Layer C) are context only. Place income is an ecological proxy, not family income; finishing school is not the full developmental pathway.

title: Ethics Training for Community Researchers Engaged in Participatory Research in Low-Income Countries: a scoping review
authors: Kelly Reger, Yasmin Ganjeh, Christine Ngaruiya, Brett Albee, Rebeca Vergara Greeno, Felipe A. Giron P., Melissa C. Funaro, Laura Bothwell, Felix Bongomin, Daniel S. Ebbs
abstract: Global shifts toward community-engagement in research emphasize the importance of ethics training for community researchers. This is particularly critical in resource-limited settings where populations can be at greater risk for research misconduct. However, current standards and frameworks for ethics training beyond the Collaborative Institutional Training Initiative (CITI) in these settings are limited. This scoping review aimed to synthesize existing literature on ethics training for community researchers engaged in participatory research in low-income countries (LICs), identify gaps, and suggest pathways toward systematic improvements. We searched MEDLINE, Embase, PsycInfo, Global Health, CINAHL, Web of Science, and Scopus, with no date or language restrictions. Data were analyzed thematically to assess the types, levels, and geographical distribution of ethics training, as well as IRB approval processes. Of 4,006 publications retrieved, 194 met inclusion criteria. A total of 44/194 (23%) studies explicitly reported ethics training for community researchers, with significant variability in training intensity. Most studies originated from Uganda (n=78), Malawi (n=35), and Ethiopia (n=20). There is a critical need for standardized, culturally appropriate ethics training frameworks for community researchers in LICs engaged in participatory research. Developing such frameworks will enhance ethical standards, promote equitable partnerships, and protect vulnerable communities from research misconduct.

title: The Beautiful Game's Class Ceiling: Who Reaches NCAA College Soccer on the Eve of the 2026 World Cup
abstract: As the United States hosts the 2026 FIFA World Cup, debate has resurfaced over whether American soccer underperforms because interest is thin or because a pay-to-play system filters out the communities most invested in the game. We analyse a near-census of one college season: 54,559 athletes on 1,826 NCAA soccer rosters in 2025–26 — 99.0% of officially sponsored programs, all three divisions, both genders — linked to Census place-level income and ethnicity and to institutional selectivity. Class and ethnicity are measured on home communities, never individuals; place measures are validated against NCAA individual aggregates. Two findings are central. Within soccer, athletes from the richest fifth of home towns reach selective colleges at roughly four times the rate of the poorest fifth (15.3% vs 3.7%; gap 11.6 pp). Men's programs field international-origin players at 2.69 times the women's rate — the largest gender asymmetry of any NCAA sport fielding both genders at scale — undiminished within the 799 institutions fielding both programs (+16.4 pp under school fixed effects). Two further results frame these: college soccer inverts the sport's global class profile, and an apparent soccer-specific selective-college gap for heavily Hispanic communities is mostly compositional under adjustment, while a modest disadvantage common to all sports survives. The census is a pre-settlement, pre-legacy baseline against which World Cup-era change can later be judged.

title: Who Says What AI Is Worth? The Division of Legitimation Labor in Enterprise AI Case Studies
abstract: Vendors of artificial intelligence publish case studies about their customers’ deployments. Each account is written by one author, the vendor, but often speaks in two voices: the vendor narrates, and a customer is quoted inside that narration. This paper reads the case study as a legitimation artifact and asks which voice is given which kind of legitimacy. The evidence is a reproducible corpus of 603 case studies from 152 vendors of AI products and services, coded into 2,242 value claims with a codebook derived from legitimacy theory, information-systems value research, and the analysis of advertising claims. Within the same case study, the two voices claim the same kinds of value. Both report gains. Both speak of realized results. What divides them is how value is expressed. The vendor’s own voice is more objective by about 48 percentage points and more quantified by about 36, claiming value as audited-looking fact, while the staged customer voice testifies from experience. The voices divide on register, not on agenda. The paper reads this as a division of legitimation labor: vendors keep cognitive legitimacy, the authority of the number, in their own voice, and borrow pragmatic and moral legitimacy through the customers they stage. The pattern makes definitional authority over AI’s worth observable and measurable, and it supplies the empirical layer that work on AI legitimation and AI washing has asserted but not measured.

title: 청년 이행체제의 시간질서와 시간자본 - 청년 이행 과정에서의 시간자본 분석
abstract: 본 연구는 청년 이행의 위기를 고용이나 소득과 같은 경제적 자원의 결핍만으로 설명하는 기존 접근의 한계를 지적하고, 시간 자체를 분석의 중심 범주로 재구성하고자 한다. 이를 위해 청년이 미래의 보상을 기대하며 현재의 시간을 선투입하고, 그 시간이 기회와 성과로 전환되거나 반대로 가치가 하락하는 과정을 설명하는 개념으로서 ‘시간자본(Time Capital)’을 제안한다. 시간자본은 비가역성, 선투입성, 전환성, 평가성, 감가 가능성이라는 다섯 가지 속성을 지니며, 타 자본의 형성과 작동을 매개하는 조건적 자원으로 기능한다. 또한 본고는 청년들이 경험하는 시간의 문제를 시간압박(주관적 경험), 시간빈곤(구조적 조건), 이행지연(생애과정적 결과)이라는 세 차원으로 구분하고, 이를 하나의 위계적 틀 속에서 통합적으로 해석한다. 시간자본의 작동은 미래 보상을 향한 압축적 선투입이 시간압박을 증폭시키고, 이것이 회복 · 관계 시간을 잠식하여 시간빈곤으로 고착되며, 궁극적으로 투입된 시간이 적절한 성과로 전환되지 못해 이행지연과 가치 감가로 이어지는 연쇄적 과정으로 설명된다. 나아가 본 연구는 이러한 소진의 결과로 나타나는 청년들의 사회적 철수나 은둔을 개인의 병리적 실패가 아니라, 시간자본 소진에 따른 합리적이고 방어적인 축소 반응으로 재해석한다. 이를 통해 청년 이행 연구의 초점을 결과 중심에서 시간질서 중심으로 전환하고, 불평등을 시간의 감가 속도와 전환 가능성의 차이로 재서술하고자 한다. 더 나아가 청년정책은 단순한 소득 · 고용 지원을 넘어, 실패 이후에도 다시 시도할 수 있는 시간 인프라를 구축하는 방향으로 재설계될 필요가 있음을 제안한다.
